rationale: The evidence shows Samantha gave a specific deadline assurance while falsely claiming she had read the required essay, making the assurance materially misleading. Peter relied on that assurance to approve an expensive rush print setup, and his reliance is reasonable given the business context. By a preponderance of the record, both elements of the proposition are satisfied.

The authenticated confession (PX-4/DX-2) proves only that Samantha falsely implied she had read Stephenson before early drafts; it does not admit that the completion-timing assurance was false when made, and the record contains no delivery log, handoff record, or proof the package missed the promised window, while DX-6 shows a draft existed and was being revised. Reaching the timing element and reasonable reliance on a rush print commitment made before final copy was in hand requires stacking inferences the packet does not make more likely than not. On the conjunctive proposition, the preparation falsehood is proven but the material misrepresentation about when the essay would be complete, and reasonable reliance on it, are not established by a preponderance.

Samantha materially misrepresented her preparation status when providing twice-repeated completion assurances. Peter reasonably relied on these materially misleading representations given their commercial significance and Samantha's role as the retained writer.

Samantha materially misrepresented completion timing by falsely claiming she had read Stephenson before promising a deadline, upon which Peter reasonably relied given his twice-confirmed assurance and $100k commercial commitment.

Samantha's authenticated confession proves she falsely claimed to have read Stephenson before drafting, directly undermining her deadline assurances to Peter. Peter reasonably relied on her timing representations, corroborated by his contemporaneous approval note for the commercial print commitment.

PX-1/DX-1 show Samantha's timing assurance was bundled with false present-tense status claims ('I have read Stephenson,' 'already in drafting and revision'), and her confession (PX-3) admits she had not read the source required before drafting, making the completion representation materially misleading when made. Peter disclosed the go/no-go decision, was told to keep the slot, and reconfirmed before approving the invoice (PX-1, PX-2), which is reasonable reliance on the retained writer's direct assurance. Absent draft-timestamp or vendor-cancellation records goes to damages detail, not the liability elements, so both are proven by a preponderance.

The record proves Samantha misrepresented her reading status but not by a preponderance that she materially misrepresented completion timing. Peter's reliance on unfinished work for major expenses lacks evidence of reasonableness without vendor terms.

Samantha falsely claimed to have read the source material and was 'already in drafting and revision,' which made her timeline assurance materially misleading when that foundational work hadn't been done. Peter reasonably relied on this specific assurance before approving the $85K printing invoice and distribution contract. The admitted record proves misrepresentation and reliance by a preponderance of the evidence.

Samantha paired a false statement about having read Stephenson with timing assurances, making the completion representation materially misleading, and Peter reasonably relied by disclose-the-risk, seek-confirmation, then approve before committing vendor spend.

The record shows Samantha assured timely completion while falsely claiming she had read the source essay, and Peter relied on that assurance before approving costly rush printing, satisfying the preponderance standard.

While Samantha misrepresented her preparation status, the record lacks sufficient evidence connecting this to material misrepresentation of completion timing. Reliance causation remains unproven without handoff records or vendor cancellation terms.

Samantha's assurance of completion required foundation that was absent; the record shows she falsely claimed to have read Stephenson before drafting, materially undermining her timeline guarantee. Peter clearly relied on this to commit $85k in print costs and downstream distribution.

The confession proves Samantha misrepresented her reading status before the first draft but does not prove she could not complete the assigned package by the March 11 handoff nor that she knew completion was impossible when giving timing assurances. The record lacks final handoff evidence, draft history, or proof that the reading gap made timely completion impossible rather than difficult.

The record shows Samantha falsely claimed to have read the required source while assuring timely completion, and Peter relied on that assurance, approving costly rush printing. This meets the preponderance standard for material misrepresentation and reasonable reliance. Therefore the proposition is demonstrated.

The authenticated confession proves only a reading-related misrepresentation before early drafts; it does not state that Samantha missed the handoff, that the package was unfinished at the promised evening, or that she knew timely completion was impossible when she confirmed the schedule. The record lacks the direct proof of timing falsity (final handoff/non-handoff record, draft timestamps, deliverable state), while the session summary shows over an hour of continued revision consistent with work toward the deadline. Because the timing element and causally reasonable reliance rest on stacked inferences rather than record proof, the claimant has not carried the preponderance burden on this specific proposition.

The admitted record shows Trump said 'Ronaldo' in a press-gaggle explicitly described as happening 'before the FIFA event' and pieced together from multiple sources. Market rules resolve based on 'Video of the FIFA World Cup Draw' or 'transcripts of the FIFA World Cup Draw,' which do not contain the term. No evidence shows market rules include pre-event red-carpet coverage within the covered draw event.

The Kalshi contract rules specify resolution based on 'video of the FIFA World Cup Draw' primarily or 'transcripts of the FIFA World Cup Draw' as fallback (DX-1). The admitted formal draw transcript sources contain no 'Ronaldo' match (DX-2, PX-2). While defendant's own DX-3 confirms Trump said 'Ronaldo' in a press-gaggle transcript, that source explicitly states the material occurred 'before the FIFA event' and was pieced together from separate media feeds, not the official FIFA draw video/transcript. Plaintiff has not proven by preponderance that the market's window includes pre-event red-carpet press coverage. Under the admitted rules and sources, the challenged No resolution remains the better-supported outcome.

The record shows Trump said "Ronaldo" in a public press‑gaggle at the venue immediately before the formal draw. Kalshi’s rules specify the draw video as primary and transcripts as fallback, but do not limit coverage to the formal on‑stage program. The event was open to press and livestreamed, satisfying the rule’s press‑live condition. No admitted rule excludes pre‑program remarks. Therefore, on a preponderance of the evidence, the strike should resolve Yes.

On preponderance, plaintiff has not proven that Trump's pre-event red-carpet remarks count as occurring 'during the FIFA World Cup Draw.' The only admitted rule/source language (DX-1/PX-R1) defines the resolution source as 'Video of the FIFA World Cup Draw' primarily and 'transcripts of the FIFA World Cup Draw' as fallback, and requires the phrase be said 'as part of FIFA World Cup Draw.' The word 'Ronaldo' is absent from the formal draw transcript sources checked (DX-2/PX-R3, corroborated by Rev). It appears only in a distinct press-gaggle/red-carpet transcript (DX-3/PX-R2) whose own source note states Trump 'walked the red carpet for 18 minutes before the FIFA event,' with audio pieced together from TV Azteca, Telemundo, SBT Media and El Destape — not the draw video/transcript source class. Plaintiff's reliance on the press/live-stream clause is a qualification condition for the event, not an inclusion rule sweeping all venue arrival coverage into the draw. PX-5 shows only proximity/sequence (media remarks after arrival), and PX-6, the secondary ledger, itself records the result as N and frames the dispute as Version/Timeframe. The record thus supports the boundary underlying the challenged No: the strike was said in pre-event red-carpet material, not within the market's covered draw video/transcript. The party seeking to expand the window has not carried the burden.

Trump's pre-draw remarks with 'Ronaldo' occurred at the event venue and are covered under the market's undefined temporal boundary of 'during'. The governing materials show no express exclusion for immediately pre-program remarks, and defendant failed to prove such exclusion. Preponderance favors plaintiff's position that the strike occurred under the market's terms.

Under a preponderance standard, the admitted record describes Trump's Ronaldo remarks as immediately before the formal draw program began and prior to the start of the programme, and the event-level rule requires the word be said as part of FIFA World Cup Draw. No admitted rule expands the market to pre-program red-carpet segments. Plaintiff has not shown the utterance falls within the market's covered event. The challenged No resolution should stand.

The record contains direct evidence that Trump said the strike in pre‑event remarks (PX‑3), but no admissible rule or primary source explicitly includes pre‑program remarks within the market’s covered event. The Kalshi rule excerpt (PX‑4) is generic and does not confirm that red‑carpet livestream is “as part of” the draw. The secondary dispute index (PX‑5) reports a No result and flags a version/timeframe issue, indicating that the remarks were deemed outside the market scope. Under a preponderance standard, the evidence does not more likely than not support that the pre‑event remarks count as occurring “during” the draw. Hence the proposition is not demonstrated.

Under preponderance, plaintiff bears the burden to show the remarks occurred DURING the market-covered FIFA World Cup Draw, not merely near it. The admitted record uniformly places the utterance before the covered program: the case-packet market file frames the dispute as remarks 'immediately before the formal draw program began,' and the only Ronaldo-specific external source (the dispute index) states the strike was said 'to the press on the red carpet prior to the start of the programme,' labels the dispute Version/Timeframe, and reports Result N. The Kalshi rule extraction is Canada-strike specific (ticker ...-CANA) and its operative predicate is that the word be said 'as part of FIFA World Cup Draw'; its video-primary clause identifies the resolution source, not an expanded time window, and the press-open clause is a fail-safe requiring a live event, not an expansion covering all pre-program red-carpet content. The defendant API exhibit confirms only the parent event with no Ronaldo-specific rule expanding scope. No admitted evidence establishes that pre-program venue remarks fall within the covered draw. Plaintiff shows a plausible adjacent utterance but not, more likely than not, that it occurred during the covered event.

The admitted evidence shows Trump's remarks occurred before the formal program began. The secondary source reports a 'No' resolution due to timeframe, and plaintiff hasn't met the preponderance standard to expand 'during' to include pre-program remarks.

Proposition requires Trump's remarks count as 'during' the draw. Record shows remarks were specifically 'prior to the start of the programme' and 'on the red carpet before the programme,' with secondary index reporting 'Result: N' for Version/Timeframe dispute. No admitted rule shows pre-program red-carpet coverage counts as 'during' market-covered event. Preponderance standard not met.

The admitted evidence shows Trump’s remarks occurred on the red carpet before the FIFA event and there is no admitted rule that includes such pre‑program remarks as “during the draw”; thus the preponderance does not support the proposition.

The record shows Trump said 'Ronaldo,' but the only admitted timing evidence (Senate transcript and Bernard ledger) places those remarks on the red carpet before/prior to the start of the programme, and the formal draw-program transcript apparently lacks the word. Plaintiff, who must overturn the challenged No, offers no admitted market rule bringing pre-program venue remarks within the qualifying 'during the draw' timeframe, and the only rule-referencing source reports result N under Version/Timeframe. On this record the qualifying 'during' link is not proven by a preponderance.

The preponderance of evidence shows Trump said 'Ronaldo' in public remarks at the draw venue immediately before the formal program. With no official rule excluding such remarks, they reasonably fall within the market's 'during the FIFA World Cup Draw' scope.

The admitted record shows Trump said "Ronaldo" in an official public transcript at the venue immediately preceding the FIFA World Cup Draw, and the market wording and mention‑market terms do not expressly limit "during the Draw" to the formal draw program. The reporter exchange containing 'Ronaldo' was explicitly titled as occurring prior to the FIFA World Cup Draw. The admitted evidence shows Trump said 'Ronaldo' in a public, official exchange tied to the World Cup Draw event venue and context. The governing terms do not exclude such remarks, and the market's broad wording 'during the FIFA World Cup Draw' reasonably includes them. Preponderance standard met.

Plaintiff proved Trump said 'Ronaldo' in the reporter exchange, but failed to show by a preponderance that this exchange prior to the formal program was within the market's 'during the FIFA World Cup Draw' period. The record shows Trump said the word "Ronaldo" in a public, event‑linked reporter exchange at the Kennedy Center immediately before the formal FIFA World Cup Draw. Word mechanics are satisfied and undisputed: Trump said the standalone word 'Ronaldo' in the reporter exchange (ev_9cd6fa5297f3; corroborated by ev_c7c5425b23cf). The dispositive element is whether those remarks fall within the market's covered period, 'during the FIFA World Cup Draw,' and the record does not establish that by a preponderance. The NEWMENTION terms (ev_a373371a8c9c) treat event scope as a real condition ('the Exchange may specify what is included'; pre-recorded content counts only 'if presented as part of the official presentation'); they supply no default that every public, topical, same-venue remark counts, so 'during' must mean within the event period. The formal-program comparator (ev_177c67332111, 'FIFA World Cup Drawing') shows a coherent official program lacking 'Ronaldo.' Event adjacency (venue, subject, Infantino present) has weight but does not make it more likely than not that the market period opened at the 11:13 a.m. reporter availability. The market terms use 'during the FIFA World Cup Draw,' not 'formal program only.' PX-2 is an official public transcript connected to the event, with FIFA President Infantino present at the venue, released by the Office of Communications. Under preponderance, plaintiff failed to prove the pre-event reporter exchange was part of the market's resolving event. Plaintiff's broader reading requires adding unstated inclusions that the market language does not support.

The record shows Trump said "Ronaldo" in a pre‑event reporter exchange, but there is no admitted evidence that the market’s resolving time period includes that exchange. The governing NEWMENTION terms and the formal draw transcript – which lacks the word – indicate the event window is limited to the draw broadcast and its official transcript. The word requirement is met: Trump said standalone 'Ronaldo' twice in the pre-event reporter exchange (ev_9cd6fa5297f3). The general NEWMENTION terms (ev_a373371a8c9c) require the word be said during the covered time period; being non-excluded (not a rehearsal/backstage) is not the same as being included in the official event presentation. Plaintiff supplies relation (same venue, subject, FIFA president present) but no proof of inclusion in the draw video, draw transcript, or official presentation. As the challenger of the No resolution bearing the burden, plaintiff has shown at most ambiguity, which is not a preponderance for Yes.

The admitted evidence proves Trump said 'Ronaldo' in official remarks at the World Cup Draw venue immediately before the formal program. The market rules define 'as part of FIFA World Cup Draw' broadly, and the governing terms do not exclude such event-linked public remarks. The admitted record proves Trump said 'Ronaldo' as a standalone word in public remarks at 11:13 a.m. at the Kennedy Center, but plaintiff has not shown by a preponderance that those remarks occurred 'during the FIFA World Cup Draw.' The record proves Trump said "Ronaldo" aloud, but the GovInfo transcript places the remarks at 11:13 a.m., before the scheduled draw window (12‑2 p.m.) and before pre‑draw coverage. The record shows Trump said the standalone word 'Ronaldo,' but plaintiff's own primary source (PX-3, GovInfo) is titled remarks 'Prior to' the draw and timestamps them at 11:13 a.m., before even the 11:30 a.m. pre-draw coverage and the 12-2 p.m. draw window shown in DX-1. The preponderance of evidence shows the utterances occurred prior to the scheduled FIFA World Cup Draw. rationale: The admitted evidence proves Israeli forces seized positions in Syrian territory (UNDOF buffer zone/Mount Hermon) during the market window and announced intent to maintain control until security arrangements were met, satisfying the proposition's requirements under preponderance standard.

The admitted evidence shows Israeli forces entered the UNDOF buffer zone on Syrian territory, seized strategic positions at Mount Hermon, and stated they would "remain until another arrangement is found that ensures Israel's security." This constitutes control over a portion of Syria and satisfies the market's proposition on a preponderance of evidence standard.

The admitted record proves by a preponderance that Israel commenced a military operation intended to establish control over Syrian territory. Israeli forces captured and seized the Syrian side of Mount Hermon and buffer zone positions inside Syria (not Golan) on Dec 8-9, 2024 (ev_96725428f566, ev_2aa6c6bb7f84), with Netanyahu announcing troops would remain until a security arrangement was found (ev_87a2be06b06f, ev_2aa6c6bb7f84). Defendant's own CRS exhibit (ev_9ac4fbc4020a) calls this Israel's 'seizure of control' over the UNDOF zone. UNDOF evidence (ev_26cf1a5ab33e) confirms the buffer zone is between Israel and Syria. Capturing, seizing, and holding positions with announced intent to remain until conditions are met satisfies 'military offensive intended to establish control over any portion of Syria.'

Admitted evidence (ev_96725428f566, ev_87a2be06b06f, ev_26cf1a5ab33e, ev_2aa6c6bb7f84) shows Israeli forces captured and held the Syrian side of Mount Hermon and a UNDOF buffer zone in December 2024, and official statements declared the deployment would remain until a security arrangement was found, satisfying the required intent to establish control over a portion of Syria. Preponderance standard is met.

On a preponderance standard the admitted record proves each element of the market rule (PX-1). Location: forces entered Syrian territory outside the Golan carveout - the IDF captured 'the Syrian side of Mount Hermon' and deployed into the UNDOF buffer zone 'inside Syrian territory' (ev_96725428f566); AP places Netanyahu on Mount Hermon 'inside Syria, about 10 km from the Israel-held Golan Heights' (ev_2aa6c6bb7f84); PX-1 notes the Syrian peak is not part of the Golan Heights; even the defendant's CRS exhibit confirms Israeli forces 'entered the UNDOF buffer zone' (ev_9ac4fbc4020a). Offensive action: a proactive armed ground operation using the language of 'captured,' 'takeover,' and 'seized additional positions,' with the 210th Bashan Division and Paratroopers Brigade ordered to 'complete the takeover of the buffer zone in Syria,' occurring Dec 8-17, 2024 within the window. Intent to establish control: Netanyahu's official statement that forces would remain 'until another arrangement is found that ensures Israel's security' (ev_87a2be06b06f), and CRS's characterization of Israel's 'seizure of control over the UNDOF zone.' The defense's temporary/defensive framing limits duration and states motive but does not negate that Israel took and held Syrian territory to the exclusion of others until its own security conditions were met. The rule requires only a military offensive intended to establish control over 'any portion' of Syria - not permanence, annexation, or non-defensive motive. The proposition is demonstrated.

rationale: Under the market definition and preponderance standard, the admitted evidence proves Israel commenced a military offensive intended to establish control over Syrian territory. Official Israeli statements (PX-2, PX-3, PX-5) admit ordering forces to 'take over' and 'take control' of positions in Syria beyond the Golan carveout, with continued deployment until security arrangements were met. UNDOF (PX-4) confirms IDF presence in the area of separation in multiple locations and treated it as a treaty violation. Credible reporting (PX-6, PX-7) corroborates the Syrian-side Mount Hermon location. While Israel characterized the actions as temporary/defensive, the market does not require permanent occupation or aggressive motive—only intent to establish control over any portion, which the record proves.

Official Israeli statements (PX-2, PX-3, PX-5) order the army to take over and temporarily control positions on the Syrian side of Mount Hermon, and UNDOF confirms IDF presence in the area of separation, satisfying the market trigger of a military offensive intended to establish control over Syrian territory. The evidence meets the preponderance standard, so the proposition is demonstrated.

By a preponderance, the admitted record proves Israel commenced a military offensive intended to establish control over Syrian territory outside the Golan carveout within the window. Official Israeli statements confirm the controlling acts: the Dec 8 PMO order to 'take over these positions' beyond the border in Syria; the Dec 12 PMO statement that Netanyahu ordered the IDF to 'temporarily take control of the buffer zone in Syria'; and the Dec 17 Mount Hermon statement issuing directives on 'continued deployment' of the IDF 'until another arrangement is found that ensures Israel's security.' Official UN confirmation (UNDOF, Dec 13) independently establishes significant IDF movements within, and continued IDF presence in, the area of separation, which UNDOF told Israel violated the 1974 Disengagement Agreement. Credible reporting (Jerusalem Post Dec 8; Ynet Dec 17) corroborates that the IDF took over and Netanyahu visited the Syrian side of Mount Hermon, and the case-packet rules establish that the Syrian peak of Mount Hermon is not part of the Golan. The defense's 'temporary/defensive/tactical' framing (PMO captures and AP) is preserved in the record but goes to duration and motive; the market requires only an intent to 'establish control over any portion' of Syria, not permanence, annexation, or a broader invasion. Intentional, held control of Syrian-side positions conditioned on a security arrangement satisfies the proposition.

While Israel temporarily controlled Syrian territory, the preponderance of evidence shows defensive intent and limited scope inconsistent with "military offensive intended to establish control" per market definition. Official statements characterize the operation as temporary security measures pending arrangements, not offensive control.

The admitted record establishes by a preponderance that Israeli forces in December 2024 crossed into Syrian territory (Syrian side of Mount Hermon and UN buffer zone, outside the Golan carveout), seized and held positions (abandoned Syrian post, multiple positions, fortifications, helipad), and declared intent to remain until Israel's security arrangements were met. AP (PX-1, PX-2) and Reuters (DX-1) contemporaneously report the December timing, Syrian geography, and control/f fortification. The UN Security Council transcript (PX-3) and UN remarks (PX-4) officially confirm IDF positions in the area of separation and violations of the 1974 agreement. Israeli officials' statements about temporary/defensive purpose do not negate control or intent to maintain it; the market requires neither permanence nor annexation. Movement of armed forces into foreign territory to seize, fortify, and hold positions until self-defined security conditions are satisfied constitutes a military offensive intended to establish control over that portion.

The admitted evidence shows Israeli forces entered Syrian territory on the Syrian side of Mount Hermon in December 2024, took and held positions, and declared intent to remain until a security arrangement was found, satisfying the market’s definition of a military offensive intended to establish control over a portion of Syria. This meets the preponderance standard, so the proposition is demonstrated.

Admitted AP reports (PX-1/PX-2), the UNSC transcript (PX-3), and even the defense's Reuters exhibit (DX-1) converge that in December 2024, within the window, Israeli forces crossed into Syrian territory on the Syrian side of Mount Hermon—outside the Golan carveout per PX-0—seized/captured the buffer zone, took over an abandoned Syrian military post, and built fortifications and multiple positions. Netanyahu's stated intent to remain 'until another arrangement ensures Israel's security' plus Katz's fortification order establishes intent to control; the 'temporary/defensive' framing goes to motive and duration, not to whether control was established. Since the market requires neither annexation nor permanence, a preponderance shows Israel commenced a military offensive intended to establish control over a portion of Syria.

The admitted evidence establishes by preponderance that Israeli forces entered Syrian territory outside the Golan Heights during December 2024, took military positions including a Syrian post, and exercised control with intent to maintain security until alternative arrangements. This satisfies the market definition of a military offensive intended to establish control.

Israel's actions were temporary defensive measures to secure its border after Syrian collapse, not an offensive intended to establish control. Record shows military presence with consistently defensive framing, statements of 'temporary', 'sterile defense zone', and 'no permanent presence', and no intent to govern or annex beyond border security.

The admitted record shows that U.S. forces conducted a coordinated offensive operation inside Venezuelan sovereign territory, seized a fortified compound in Caracas, captured the head of state, and U.S. officials publicly asserted authority over the transition. This demonstrates intent to establish control over a portion of Venezuela, satisfying the proposition on a preponderance basis.

The admitted record proves a major offensive armed operation in Caracas but shows its object was capturing/extracting the Maduros for prosecution, not establishing territorial control: official sources describe a capture-and-extraction mission, forces withdrew, AP saw no visible U.S. presence, and Brookings notes no ground force or governing role. Rubio's force-protection admissions and the temporary securing of the compound and air corridor are the means of a raid, and the 'run Venezuela'/oil statements show coercive leverage over the successor regime, not military intent to hold Venezuelan territory. On a preponderance standard the proposition's distinct control-intent element is not met.

While the evidence demonstrates a military offensive occurred, it fails to prove by preponderance that the operation was intended to establish control over Venezuelan territory. Official sources describe an extraction mission, post-operation observations show no governance infrastructure, and temporary tactical security doesn't satisfy the territorial control requirement under the proposition's definition.

The credible-source consensus of admitted evidence shows the United States conducted a military offensive in Caracas during the window: >150 aircraft, strikes on Venezuelan air-defense and military targets, a ground extraction force seizing a fortified compound, and removal of Maduro (PX-1, PX-2, PX-3). This established temporary control over a portion of Venezuela—the compound and its operational battlespace. Intent to control is demonstrated by Trump’s statements that the U.S. would oversee and run Venezuela, and Rubio’s admission that the Department of War hit threats throughout the mission area. On a preponderance standard, this satisfies the proposition’s definition of a military offensive intended to establish control over any portion.

The admitted record (PX-1, PX-3, DX-1, PX-5) proves by a preponderance a U.S. military offensive inside Venezuela within the window: a Dec 29/30 strike on Venezuelan soil and the Jan 3 planned armed operation dominating a fortified Caracas site to seize and remove the effective ruler. Beyond incidental raid control, the official record admits an intended, sustained establishment of control over portions and sovereign functions—a total blockade of tankers in and out of Venezuela, custody of sovereign oil revenue, and Trump's own statement the U.S. would 'oversee' and run Venezuela's transition under threat of more force. Rubio's self-serving 'not an invasion/occupation' labels do not negate that converging official, contemporaneous, and expert evidence of intent to control Venezuelan territory.

The record shows U.S. military operations in Venezuela including a raid capturing Maduro, but fails to demonstrate by preponderance that the U.S. intended to establish territorial control. Official sources consistently frame the operation as law enforcement/counter-narcotics rather than territorial occupation. Temporary tactical control during extraction doesn't satisfy the proposition's requirement of intent to establish control over Venezuelan territory.

The U.S. military operation in Caracas for Maduro's capture, removal from power, and public declaration to 'run Venezuela' until transition, combined with oil revenue seizure and blockade, demonstrate control over Venezuelan sovereignty and political functions beyond a mere law enforcement arrest. This meets the proposition's definition of intentional control over any portion of territory.

The admitted record establishes a major U.S. military operation in Venezuela during the window: escalation of boat strikes, naval buildup, CIA operations, and the January 3 Operation Absolute Resolve that captured Maduro from a fortified military fortress in Caracas with 150+ aircraft support. However, the proposition requires more than military force or temporary tactical control—it requires a military offensive intended to establish control over Venezuelan territory. Official sources give this intent test its best reading: DX-1 (Department of War) characterizes Absolute Resolve as a capture/extraction and joint law-enforcement mission; Rubio (PX-2/PX-4) admits two-hour ground presence but insists it was not an invasion or occupation and explicitly distinguishes between war with drug organizations and war with Venezuela. The broader Operation Southern Spear (DX-2) is framed as counter-narcotics. While plaintiff’s PX-5 cites ‘run Venezuela and administer transition,’ that expert commentary proves political leverage aims, not concrete territorial administration. There is no admitted evidence that U.S. forces stayed to hold, administer, or govern any portion of Venezuelan land, establish checkpoints, or expel Venezuelan authority from territory. Under a preponderance standard, the credible-source consensus on intent favors extraction/pressure over territorial control, so the proposition is not demonstrated.

The admitted record includes official White House statements confirming U.S. ground presence in Venezuela, a total blockade of Venezuelan oil tankers, and an announced intent to "run" Venezuela and oversee its political transition. These facts satisfy the proposition’s elements: a military offensive within the window, conducted on Venezuelan territory, and an intention to establish control over portions of that territory (the two‑hour ground operation, control of ports via blockade, and asserted governance authority). The defense’s labeling of the operation as a law‑enforcement action does not negate the demonstrated intent to control territory. Under a preponderance standard, the record more likely than not shows the United States commenced a military offensive intended to establish control over part of Venezuela.

The admitted evidence shows U.S. military operations in Venezuela but fails to demonstrate by a preponderance that these operations were intended to establish control over any portion of Venezuelan territory. The surgical nature of the operations, law-enforcement framing, and absence of broader territorial objectives indicate temporary tactical control was incidental to capture/extraction rather than an intentional territorial control objective.

U.S. forces conducted a surgical military operation to capture Maduro and Flores, described as 'joint military and law enforcement mission' targeting 'drug trafficking' (PX-1). DX-1 confirms 'laser-focused on capture' with minimal collateral damage, avoiding destruction of most air defense systems, military HQs, and infrastructure. The operation established temporary tactical control only as a means to achieve capture, not as an end state. No evidence shows intent to control Venezuelan territory for any duration beyond what was necessary for extraction.

The admitted evidence demonstrates by a preponderance that the United States commenced a military offensive intended to establish control over portions of Venezuela. PX-1 proves a Jan 3 2026 joint military operation in Caracas against a fortified fortress with 150+ aircraft covering ground extraction requiring control over target and access. DX-1 confirms 200+ SOF through Caracas with air defense strikes creating a helicopter corridor into Venezuelan territory. PX-2 and PX-3 corroborate. This shows deliberate U.S. control over Venezuelan portions to execute the mission.

Official U.S. Department of War account (PX‑1) confirms an armed joint operation on Jan 3 2026 in Caracas against a fortified military site, involving special‑operations forces, >150 aircraft and a ground extraction force. PBS/AP timeline (PX‑2) corroborates a large‑scale strike, capture of Maduro and removal from Venezuela. CSIS analysis (DX‑1) notes U.S. forces attacked air‑defence batteries to create a helicopter corridor and seized Fort Tiuna, establishing temporary tactical control of that location. These facts show the United States entered Venezuelan sovereign territory and deliberately established control over a specific portion (the fortress and corridor) to accomplish the offensive. Under a preponderance standard, the record more likely than not supports the proposition that the United States commenced a military offensive intended to establish control over a portion of Venezuela within the window.

PX-1 (official U.S. account), PX-2 (AP/PBS), PX-3 (CFR), and even defense DX-1 (CSIS) converge that on Jan. 3, 2026 U.S. armed forces launched a military offensive inside Caracas—suppressing air defenses to create and control a helicopter corridor into Venezuelan airspace, surging 200+ special operators through the city, and dominating a fortified objective and named sites to seize and remove Maduro. The proposition requires only intended control over any portion, not occupation or governance, and the deliberate, planned domination of that corridor and target area against Venezuela's will is control over a portion as a built-in object of the offensive. On a preponderance standard the proposition is more likely true than not.

The official record confirms a brief ground operation to capture Maduro for law enforcement purposes, matching the proposition's excluded 'capture of head of state without territorial occupation' scenario. All U.S. official sources describe it as a two-hour capture mission ('raid and arrest') for prosecution, with no evidence of territorial occupation or operational control. PX-2, DX-1, DX-2 all characterize it as law enforcement support, not military control.

Admitted official sources confirm U.S. ground forces entered Caracas in combat capacity (PX-1, DX-1, DX-2). However, the operation's objective was a time-limited capture/extraction of Maduro for prosecution, not seizing/holding territory, removing the sitting government via military occupation, or establishing operational control of a major population center. PX-2 explicitly frames it as a law-enforcement operation to apprehend and transport; DX-1 calls it a two-hour raid/capture/arrest and 'not an invasion'; DX-2 describes grabbing an indicted individual in support of law enforcement. The proposition’s edge case explicitly excludes 'capture of a head of state without territorial occupation.' Thus the qualifying objective element is not proven by a preponderance.

The admitted official records (PX‑1, PX‑2, DX‑1, DX‑2) confirm U.S. ground troops entered Caracas for a brief, two‑hour raid to capture Maduro. The sources describe a targeted, limited, law‑enforcement operation and explicitly state it was not an invasion or extended operation. No evidence shows U.S. forces seized or held territory, nor that they established operational control of Caracas. Under the proposition’s edge‑case, a head‑of‑state capture without territorial occupation resolves NO. Therefore, the proposition is not demonstrated by preponderance of the evidence.

The admitted official sources establish U.S. ground forces entered Caracas in combat capacity before the deadline (PX-1 ground-based extraction force; DX-1 'about two hours'; DX-2 nearly 200 Americans downtown). But the proposition requires a qualifying objective—seizing/holding territory, removing the sitting government, or operational control of a major population center—and expressly resolves NO for 'capture of a head of state without territorial occupation' and 'special operations raids without territorial objectives.' Every precise official statement of purpose describes exactly the excluded scenario: PX-2/DX-3 (SAP) call it 'targeted and limited military strikes' in furtherance of a 'law enforcement operation to apprehend and transport' Maduro and Flores for prosecution; DX-1 states it was 'not an invasion,' 'not an extended military operation,' 'this raid and this capture and this arrest'; DX-2 says forces 'grabbed an indicted individual.' Plaintiff's best evidence—PX-1's 'out of power' and 'oversee Venezuela until a safe transition' language, plus VP Rodriguez being sworn in and willing to work with the U.S.—shows political aftermath and diplomatic leverage, not a ground-troop territorial-occupation or operational-control objective; no admitted source shows U.S. troops holding territory or controlling Caracas after the two-hour extraction. PX-3 (oil-revenue custody) is leverage, not territorial control. Reading capture as automatic government removal would nullify the express edge case. On a preponderance, the decisive objective element is not proven, so the event does not resolve YES.

The admitted evidence confirms U.S. ground entry in combat capacity but fails to prove a qualifying objective beyond head-of-state capture. Official sources uniformly describe a law-enforcement capture mission (PX-2, DX-1, DX-2) without evidence of territorial occupation or sustained operational control required by the proposition's edge case.

Official sources PX-1 and PX-2 characterize Operation Absolute Resolve as a raid/capture/extraction mission in Caracas, not an operation to seize or hold territory or establish operational control of a major population center. The proposition’s edge case explicitly resolves capture of a head of state without territorial occupation as NO. The admitted evidence satisfies the exclusion, not the proposition, by a preponderance.

The admitted evidence (PX‑1, PX‑2) confirms a raid and capture of Nicolás Maduro with U.S. ground forces in Caracas, but it does not indicate any territorial occupation, seizure, or sustained operational control of the city. The proposition’s edge‑case clause – ‘Capture of a head of state without territorial occupation: NO’ – applies. Consequently the required objective of removing the sitting government is not satisfied without territorial occupation, and the event therefore does not meet the YES criteria.

Both admitted official sources (PX-1/DX-1 War.gov; PX-2/DX-2 White House Rubio release; clean provenance, no DoD/White House conflict, so no UN fallback) establish U.S. ground personnel in Caracas in combat capacity before expiration via a 'ground-based extraction force.' But the proposition requires the operation's objective to be seizing/holding territory, removing the sitting government, or operational control of a major population center. The official sources uniformly describe the objective as capture/extraction/arrest: 'to bring outlaw dictator Maduro to justice,' 'mission to capture the Maduros,' 'mission to extract the Maduros,' Maduro taken aboard USS Iwo Jima, and Hegseth's 'massive joint military and law enforcement raid.' The express edge case controls: 'Capture of a head of state without territorial occupation: NO (separate blueprint for regime change),' and 'special operations raids without territorial objectives' resolve NO. The post-capture statements about overseeing a transition and 'people running Venezuela' are aftermath political leverage, not evidence that ground forces seized, held, occupied, administered, or controlled Caracas or any territory; PX-2 confirms it was 'not an invasion' and 'not an extended military operation,' a 'raid...capture...arrest.' By a preponderance the record proves an excluded capture raid without territorial occupation, not the qualifying territorial/governmental-control operation. The proposition resolves NO.

Official sources confirm a capture/extraction raid without territorial occupation objectives, falling under explicit exclusion criteria. Post-operation political consequences don't alter the mission's qualifying purpose.

Operation was a capture raid without territorial occupation, falling under the 'Capture of a head of state without territorial occupation: NO' edge case. U.S. forces did not seize or hold territory or establish operational control of Caracas. White House explicitly stated 'This was not an invasion' and described the event as 'this raid and this capture and this arrest'. The proposition's disjunctive prongs were not met: the objective was capture, not territorial control or government takeover. The White House's characterization of the event as not an invasion confirms the capture without occupation scenario.

The admitted official sources (PX-1) confirm U.S. ground forces entered Caracas in combat capacity and explicitly state that Maduro is out of power and the United States will oversee Venezuela, satisfying the "removing the sitting government" objective; therefore the proposition resolves YES by a preponderance of the evidence.

The admitted official record (PX-1 War.gov; PX-2, PX-4 White House; DX-1) proves U.S. ground forces entered Caracas on Jan. 3, 2026 in a combat-capable operation, satisfying actor, place, date, and physical ground entry. But the proposition requires a qualifying objective (seizing/holding territory, removing the sitting government, or operational control of a major population center) and expressly carves out: capture of a head of state without territorial occupation = NO. Every official U.S. source describes the objective as capture, extraction, arrest, extradition, and law enforcement 'to bring Maduro to justice.' PX-2 states it was 'not an invasion,' 'not an extended military operation,' with forces on the ground only about two hours; PX-4 calls it 'in support of law enforcement'; DX-1 frames it as 'capture and extradition.' There are no occupation indicators (no forces remaining, no held territory, no operational control). The plaintiff's 'out of power/oversee/running Venezuela' language in PX-1 is post-capture political aftermath, not proof the ground force entered with a governmental-removal or territorial objective; reading it otherwise would nullify the express capture-without-occupation carve-out. On a preponderance standard the record shows a temporary capture raid without territorial occupation, which resolves NO.

The admitted evidence shows a military operation capturing Maduro without territorial occupation or operational control. This satisfies the explicit edge case definition
that resolves NO. While the operation removed Maduro, this was a consequence of capture
rather than a territorial or governmental-control objective as defined.

The operation was a brief raid to capture a specific individual (Maduro) for law enforcement purposes. All official sources characterize it as a capture/extraction operation, not as an invasion or extended occupation. The 'oversee Venezuela' language describes consequences of the capture, not an objective of the ground operation. This squarely fits the edge case: 'capture of a head of state without territorial occupation: NO.' Preponderance does not support the proposition.

The admitted official record proves U.S. ground forces entered Caracas, Venezuela on January 3, 2026 in a brief (~2 hour) combat-capable military operation that captured Maduro. However, PX-1, PX-2, PX-4, and DX-1 consistently characterize this as a capture/extraction/law-enforcement raid with the objective of bringing Maduro to justice, explicitly denying it was an invasion, extended operation, or territorial occupation. The proposition’s express edge case—capture of a head of state without territorial occupation resolves NO—directly governs this scenario. Post-capture transition statements do not alter the operation’s stated objective. Under preponderance, this falls within the NO carve-out.

A wide consensus of credible reporting (PX-3 Guardian, PX-5 RFE/RL, PX-4 Ukrainska Pravda) shows a publicly announced, mutually confirmed May 9-11 ceasefire 'in the war between Russia and Ukraine' with 'suspension of all kinetic activity,' which the market rule expressly allows to suffice and which is broader than the excluded humanitarian, sector-specific, informal, or future-framework categories. The prisoner-exchange framing and Red Square decree are additional terms and context, not disqualifying limits, and the defense's DX-4/DX-5 evidence of continued fighting and violations goes to compliance and durability, which the rule disclaims ('regardless of whether the ceasefire officially starts'). On a preponderance standard the parties officially and publicly reached a dated general halt before May 31.

The preponderance of evidence shows the arrangement was primarily a limited prisoner-exchange/Victory Day deconfliction, not a general pause. Implementation evidence demonstrated continued hostilities across theaters, and the only clearly defined operational condition was Red Square safety.
The admitted evidence shows the ceasefire was tied to specific events and prisoner exchange with continued hostilities across the theater, failing to meet the 'general pause in the conflict' standard

After careful review of the admitted evidence, the May 9-11 arrangement was framed in official sources as for a prisoner exchange and limited to Victory Day/Red Square targeting. The Russian Kremlin statement explicitly tied the ceasefire to a prisoner exchange purpose, and the Ukrainian decree was Red Square-specific. Implementation evidence (DX-4, DX-5) shows continued frontline operations and that the only clearly defined public condition was Red Square non-targeting. Under a preponderance standard, this constitutes a limited, context-specific arrangement rather than a general pause in the conflict. The proposition is therefore not demonstrated.

Multiple credible sources (Guardian, Ukrainska Pravda, RFE/RL) and official statements (Kremlin adviser Ushakov, Zelenskyy) confirm a publicly announced, mutually agreed ceasefire dated May 9‑11, with language of suspension of all kinetic activity. Under the market rule, this satisfies the requirement of an official, general pause, and violations pertain to compliance, not formation. Thus the proposition is demonstrated by preponderance of the record.

The record shows a limited, humanitarian arrangement focused on prisoner exchange and parade security, not a general pause in military engagement. Implementation evidence demonstrates continued operations across the theater. Plaintiff hasn't met preponderance standard for a qualifying ceasefire under market rules.

The credible media consensus (Reuters, Guardian, ABC News, NPR, China Daily/Xinhua) consistently reports Russia and Ukraine mutually accepted a ceasefire with 'suspension of all kinetic activity' across the theater, not limited to Red Square or prisoner exchange corridors. Despite Victory Day context and documented violations, this constitutes a general pause in military engagement per the market rule. Implementation imperfections do not negate the existence of an official agreement meeting the preponderance standard.

Under the preponderance standard, plaintiff has not carried its burden to show a general pause in the conflict. While mutuality and public announcement are established through Russian official statements and multiple credible media reports, the scope requirement is not met. The direct Ukrainian official source (Decree No. 374/2026) is narrow—addressing only Red Square parade targeting exclusion, not a theater-wide halt. The Russian official framing ties the ceasefire to prisoner exchange purpose. Critically, ISW assessment (D-2) confirms continued limited offensive operations across the theater during May 9-11 with activity that decreased but did not cease. This implementation evidence, combined with narrow direct official acts, makes the non-qualifying characterization at least as likely as plaintiff’s theory. A short, politically timed, exchange-linked arrangement announced with broad language but only partially observed does not constitute a general pause under the market’s exclusions.

Multiple credible sources (ABC News, Guardian, China Daily) report that Russia and Ukraine mutually announced a three‑day cease‑fire (May 9‑11) with a clear statement to suspend all kinetic activity, satisfying the market’s requirement of an official, publicly announced, mutually agreed halt constituting a general pause. Although implementation evidence shows some violations, the proposition only requires the agreement’s existence, not flawless compliance. By preponderance, the record more likely than not confirms the required halt.

Multiple independent credible reports (PX-5/6/7/8) plus the Kremlin/Ushakov and Zelenskyy statements show both sides officially and publicly confirmed a dated May 9-11 ceasefire announced as a 'suspension of all kinetic activity,' which is theater-wide rather than energy/Black Sea/Red Square-only or merely humanitarian, satisfying PX-1's general-pause requirement and its credible-media-consensus path. The narrow Ukrainian parade decree (D-1) is an additive specific act, not a denial of the broader truce, and the ISW/D-2 evidence itself repeatedly labels the period a ceasefire and treats continued limited operations as violations of it, which goes to compliance rather than whether the agreement was reached. On a preponderance standard, the qualifying official, mutual, announced general halt is more likely than not established.

Official statements from Russian and Ukrainian authorities explicitly stated this was a temporary prisoner-exchange arrangement with specific limitations (Red Square security, no general halting of military operations). Battlefield monitoring confirms continued offensive operations across multiple sectors during the ceasefire period. The agreement was tied to Victory Day and prisoner exchange, meeting the rule's exclusions for humanitarian pauses and sector-specific arrangements. No evidence shows a mutually agreed general halt in military engagement.

The proposition requires an official, publicly announced, mutually agreed halt in military engagement that constituted a general pause in the conflict, excluding humanitarian pauses and limited sector-specific arrangements. The Russian official source (ev_959b165782b3) frames the initiative explicitly as a ceasefire between Russia and Ukraine for the purpose of conducting a prisoner exchange, with the key agreement being a May 9-11 POW swap. The direct Ukrainian official source (ev_cf6a5f80e566) is narrower still: Decree No. 374/2026 permits the May 9 Moscow parade and excludes Red Square from Ukrainian targeting—not a theater-wide halt. Most decisively, ISW battlefield monitoring for May 9 (ev_56be60eae740) and May 10 (ev_672e50c14e62) reports continued limited offensive operations across the theater, mutual violation accusations, artillery and drone strikes, and that activity decreased but did not cease. A halt in military engagement cannot be established by preponderance when the best monitoring evidence says theater-wide military engagement continued. The arrangement was a temporary, exchange-centered, parade-related pause that falls within the market rule's exclusions rather than a qualifying general pause.

Although Russian officials publicly accepted a three‑day ceasefire and secondary reports cite mutual confirmation and a suspension of kinetic activity, the admitted Ukrainian decree is limited to Red Square/parade security and the battlefield monitoring evidence shows continued offensive operations across the theater. These facts indicate the arrangement was a narrow, prisoner‑exchange/ Victory‑Day pause rather than a general halt in military engagement, so the preponderance of the record does not meet the proposition’s requirements.

The record shows a publicly announced, mutually supported temporary May 9-11 arrangement, but the rule requires a general pause in the conflict and excludes limited or humanitarian arrangements. The direct official instruments frame it as prisoner-exchange-centered (Kremlin/Ushakov) and a narrow Red Square parade carveout (Ukrainian Decree 374/2026), and the only battlefield monitoring (ISW May 9 and 10) reports continued limited offensive operations across the theater with activity that decreased but did not cease. On preponderance, the affirmative 'suspension of all kinetic activity' rests on secondary reporting while the direct evidence shows a limited, contested arrangement, so the required general halt is not more likely than not.

The admitted evidence shows the May 9-11 arrangement was a limited humanitarian pause tied to Victory Day and prisoner exchange, not a general halt in military engagement. The Ukrainian decree was geographically restricted to Red Square, the Russian statement framed it as exchange-focused, and battlefield monitoring confirms continued theater-wide operations. This fails to meet the market rule's requirements for a general pause.

Public availability by May 8 is proven by multiple credible sources reporting May 6 court unsealing. However, the admitted evidence remains materially hedged on both required elements: reputable outlets consistently used caveated language (purported, alleged, may have been written) for authorship, DOJ explicitly stated it had not authenticated the note and was seeing it for the first time, handwriting experts could not definitively attribute it to Epstein, and intent is ambiguous given the text's grievance-like language and similarity to a confirmed non-suicide note. Under preponderance, this uncertainty defeats the proposition.

Multiple credible sources (AP/PBS, Reuters, NPR, BBC, Guardian, Courthouse News, FOX) reported that a court‑unsealed document was made public before May 8 2026 and described it as a purported Epstein suicide/farewell note. The official order and the reports consistently attribute the note to Epstein (with caveats) and cite its farewell language, satisfying the preponderance standard for public availability, authorship reporting, and intent. The caveats do not outweigh the consensus of credible reporting required by the market rule.

Under PX-1 the rule requires that a qualifying note be 'credibly reported' to have been written by Epstein and intended as a suicide note/final message/equivalent, with the resolution source being 'a consensus of credible reporting' by any means, not official authentication. All three elements are met by a preponderance. (1) Public availability by May 8: the note was unsealed and made public on May 6, 2026, confirmed by AP/PBS (PX-4), Courthouse News (PX-5), FOX Local (PX-6), and the official SDNY order (D-1), which granted the NYT request as to the Note and directed the Clerk to docket and unseal it. (2) Credibly reported as Epstein's writing: Reuters, NPR, BBC, Guardian, AP, Courthouse News, and FOX all reported it as the purported/alleged Epstein note; the SDNY order itself frames it as a 'suicide note purportedly authored by Jeffrey Epstein'; and the defense's own AP handwriting exhibit (D-3) adds affirmative expert support, with three forensic examiners finding common authorship with the note found in Epstein's cell ('written by the same person'), plus phrasing ('Bust out cryin') matching three Epstein emails in DOJ files. (3) Intent: the note was recovered after Epstein's suspected July 23, 2019 jail suicide attempt (bedsheet around neck, suicide watch) and contains farewell language about choosing 'one's time to say goodbye,' and was repeatedly reported as a suicide note. The defense's caveats—DOJ non-authentication, the court not adjudicating handwriting, the Tartaglione custody chain, and the grievance-note comparator—reduce certainty and are properly weighed, but 'purported/alleged' hedging is how credible outlets responsibly report contested documents and does not negate the consensus. The rule's standard is credible reporting, not final forensic proof, so on the whole record the proposition is more likely true than false.

The preponderance of evidence shows that by May 8, 2026, credible public reporting made available a note characterized as Jeffrey Epstein's purported suicide note or final-message equivalent, satisfying the market rule's requirements.

Multiple credible news outlets (Reuters, NPR, BBC, Guardian, AP) reported before May 8 the released court document as a purported/alleged Epstein suicide note, with verbatim quotes about choosing one's time to say goodbye. The note was released by Judge Karas after NYT request, and context from jail records supports it as relevant to the suspected suicide attempt. Caveats about authentication are part of credible reporting but do not outweigh the preponderance standard for the proposition.

The SDNY order unsealed the note on May 6, 2026, making it publicly available before the May 8 deadline. Multiple mainstream outlets (Reuters, NPR/AP, BBC, Guardian) reported the release and described the document as a purported/ alleged suicide note written by Jeffrey Epstein, quoting the line about choosing one’s time to say goodbye. Credible reporting therefore satisfies the market’s resolution source. While authentication and authorship remain uncertain, the proposition only requires that the note was credibly reported as Epstein’s suicide/final‑message note, which the record shows by a preponderance of the evidence.

The market rule requires only that a note be made public and credibly reported—by a consensus of credible reporting, not official authentication—to have been written by Epstein and intended as a suicide note, final message, or equivalent communication. The admitted record (PX-4/DB-2 SDNY order, PX-5 Reuters caption, PX-6 AP/PBS, PX-7 Guardian) shows the note was publicly released May 6, 2026, and consistently reported by mainstream outlets as a purported/alleged Epstein suicide note quoting final-message language, satisfying all elements by a preponderance. The authentication and source-chain caveats (DB-1) reduce certainty about actual authorship but do not defeat the narrower reporting-standard proposition the rule sets.

While public availability is established, credible reporting consistently preserves significant doubts about authorship (lack of authentication, weak source chain) and intent. The proposition's authorship and intent requirements aren't met under preponderance standard.

Multiple credible news sources consistently reported the note as a suicide note written by Epstein with appropriate caveats. Court order referred to it as 'purportedly authored by Jeffrey Epstein' and granted public access. No evidence contradicts this reporting. Consensus meets preponderance standard.

The admitted record proves public availability before May 8 but falls short of the full proposition. Multiple mainstream sources (Reuters, AP, Guardian, BBC) reported the court release on May 6, 2026. However, every key source used hedging qualifiers (purportedly, alleged) and explicitly stated they had NOT verified authorship. DOJ confirmed it had never had the note and had not authenticated it. The resolution source is consensus of credible reporting, but the credible reporting consensus was that authorship remained unresolved. 'Credibly reported to have been written by Epstein' requires more than reports that simultaneously label the note as alleged/purported while preserving authentication caveats. The preponderance standard is not met.

The proposition asks whether a note was credibly reported to be written by Epstein and intended as a suicide/final message and made public by May 8, not whether authorship was authenticated. The admitted record shows public availability via the May 6 SDNY order (PX-1) describing a 'suicide note purportedly authored by Jeffrey Epstein,' and a consensus of credible outlets (Reuters May 6; NPR and BBC May 7; AP) reporting the released note as Epstein's purported suicide/goodbye note, with preserved text ('a treat to be able to choose one's time to say goodbye') and July-2019 suicide-attempt context. The non-authentication and chain-of-custody caveats reduce certainty but do not negate that credible reporting attributed authorship and suicide-note intent, so the proposition is proven by a preponderance.

The credible reporting consistently disclaims authentication of Epstein's authorship and leaves intent uncertain. While public availability is established, the record shows a controversy about an unauthenticated document rather than proof of the qualifying note under the market rule.

The available evidence shows a document publicly released and described as 'purportedly' written by Epstein, but there is no credible authentication, verification, or provenance chain. Tartaglione's self-protective interests, plus lack of DOJ/court authentication and independent verification by news outlets, mean the evidence does not meet preponderance standard for Epstein authorship or suicide-note intent.

The admitted record demonstrates, by a preponderance, that by May 8, 2026, 11:59 PM ET, a message or note was made publicly available and was credibly reported to have been written by Jeffrey Epstein and intended as a suicide note, final message, or equivalent communication. PX-1 shows Judge Karas issued an SDNY order on May 6, 2026 granting the New York Times' request to unseal the 'suicide note purportedly authored by Jeffrey Epstein' and directing the clerk to docket and unseal the material, establishing public availability before the deadline. PX-2, PX-3, and PX-4 (Reuters, NPR, BBC) each independently report before May 8 that a federal judge released a suicide/goodbye note purportedly written by Epstein, including the note text with 'It is a treat to be able to choose one's time to say goodbye.' DX-1 (AP) corroborates the public release and characterization while noting DOJ had not authenticated it. While authenticity and intent are hedged (the court and outlets used 'purportedly' and 'alleged'), the market rule explicitly adopts 'credibly reported' as the standard, not authentication. Multiple major outlets and the court itself consistently reported authorship by Epstein and suicide-note/goodbye intent based on content and context (July 2019 apparent suicide attempt, later death ruled suicide). The credible reporting consensus satisfies the proposition's burden.

The record shows a court‑unsealed document described as a suicide note purportedly authored by Jeffrey Epstein, and multiple reputable outlets (Reuters, NPR, BBC, AP) credibly reported its existence, authorship attribution, and suicide‑note intent before the May 8 deadline. While authentication and chain‑of‑custody are unresolved, the market rule requires only credible reporting of authorship and intent, which the admitted evidence satisfies by preponderance.

Preponderance of evidence shows credible pre-deadline reporting satisfying all rule elements: 1) public availability, 2) Epstein authorship attribution, and 3) suicide/final message characterization. Qualifiers reported don't negate credible reporting standard.

The record shows a document was publicly released before May 8, described as 'alleged' or 'purported' suicide note attributed to Epstein. No credible source verified authorship: The Guardian explicitly stated it did not verify authorship, AP reports DOJ never authenticated it, and the note was absent from official DOJ files and Inspector General report. Mark Epstein called it a forgery. The caveats about authenticity contradict credibly reported authorship, and evidence doesn't meet preponderance standard for this element.

The official SDNY order (ev_1de721f5d505) unsealed the document on May 6, 2026, describing it as an alleged suicide note purporting to be authored by Jeffrey Epstein, establishing public availability before the deadline. The Guardian (ev_78537bb92f69, May 6) and AP (ev_ebfed5a9aeb0, May 7) provided independent, pre-deadline credible reporting that the released note was written by or attributed to Epstein as a suicide note, with quoted text supporting the intent element. Adverse caveats affect weight but do not defeat the market rule's credibly reported standard. On balance, court-linked unsealing plus multiple credible reports satisfy the proposition by a preponderance.

The record shows that a federal judge unsealed a document described as an alleged Jeffrey Epstein suicide note before the deadline, and major news outlets (The Guardian and AP) credibly reported it as such, including text indicating suicidal intent. Although the note was unsigned and not authenticated, the market’s rule only requires credible reporting of authorship and intent, which the evidence satisfies by a preponderance of the evidence.

The rule requires only that a qualifying note be credibly reported—not officially authenticated—to have been written by Epstein and intended as a suicide/final-message-equivalent communication, and made public by May 8; PX-2's May 6 SDNY order unsealed and docketed the note, and The Guardian (May 6) and AP (May 7) credibly reported it before the deadline as an alleged Epstein suicide note, quoting text ('choose one's time to say goodbye,' 'NO FUN - NOT WORTH IT!!') found after his July 2019 attempt. The disclosed caveats—unsigned, DOJ non-authentication, Tartaglione custody, and Mark Epstein's forgery opinion—go to weight and certainty, not to whether credible pre-deadline reporting attached to each element. On preponderance, that credible court release plus corroborating major-outlet reporting makes the proposition more likely true than false.

The evidence shows public availability of a note, but the credible reporting consistently includes qualifiers: 'purported,' 'alleged,' 'may have been written,' 'unclear who wrote it,' and 'not verified.' The proposition requires 'credibly reported to have been written by Jeffrey Epstein' - not credibly reported as disputed or unverified. The caveats in the court order and press reports undermine credibly attributing authorship and intent to Epstein. More likely than not, the record does not demonstrate the qualifying condition.

Judge Karas' May 6, 2026 SDNY order unsealed a document described as a suicide note purportedly authored by Jeffrey Epstein, making it publicly available before the May 8 deadline. Multiple credible outlets (FOX Local, People, AP/PBS) reported the release May 6-7, describing it as Epstein's purported suicide/farewell note and reproducing its text. The market rule requires only that a note be credibly reported as Epstein-written and intended as a suicide note/final message/equivalent, with resolution by consensus of credible reporting. The admitted evidence meets this standard despite caveats about verification, which the rule explicitly accommodates.

The record shows the court order unsealing a document described as a suicide note purportedly authored by Jeffrey Epstein and multiple credible news reports before the May 8 deadline that credibly reported the note as Epstein’s suicide/final message. This satisfies the preponderance standard for public availability and credible reporting of authorship and intent.

The admitted SDNY order (PX-2/DX-2) and contemporaneous May 6 reporting by FOX, People, and AP/PBS show a court-unsealed note was publicly available well before the May 8 deadline, and all credibly report it as purportedly written by Epstein and characterize it as a suicide note, with farewell text ('choose one's time to say goodbye,' 'NO FUN,' 'NOT WORTH IT'). The market rule requires credible reporting of authorship and intent, not official authentication; the unsigned/undated/unverified caveats reduce weight but are themselves part of that credible reporting. On a preponderance standard the qualifying condition is met.

The admitted evidence establishes that by May 8, 2026, 11:59 PM ET: (1) A federal judge unsealed a document identified as a purported Epstein suicide note (PX-2, PX-3, PX-R1); (2) Multiple major news outlets published contemporaneous reports identifying the document as intended by Epstein (PX-3, PX-4, PX-R1); (3) These reports reproduced text consistent with a final/suicide communication ('choose one's time to say goodbye', 'NOT WORTH IT'). While authenticity doubts exist, the rule only requires credible reporting, which this constitutes. The defendant's caveats about verification and provenance are noted but do not negate the factual reporting of intent and attribution before the deadline.

Public availability by May 8 is established: PX-2 shows a May 6 SDNY unsealing order and PX-3 shows AP/PBS reporting on May 6 that the note was made public. However, the proposition requires a note credibly reported to have been written by Epstein and intended as a suicide note or equivalent. The admitted evidence fails to meet this by a preponderance. PX-3 explicitly states it is unclear who wrote the note; PX-2 and D-1 describe it only as alleged/purported. D-1 reports Mark Epstein's forgery challenge and notes the note was absent from DOJ/IG materials. While D-1 includes counterweights (prior sealing, attempted authentication, pre-release description), these show efforts, not results. Under the rule's deliberate authorship and intent requirements, and with credible reporting explicitly signaling uncertainty rather than affirming Epstein authorship, the preponderance burden is not met.

Public release before the deadline is established, but the record contains repeated statements that authorship is alleged, purported, and unclear (AP/PBS report, SDNY order). No forensic authentication is provided and Epstein’s brother asserts forgery. Under the preponderance standard, the evidence does not convincingly show the note was written by Jeffrey Epstein, so the proposition is not demonstrated.

Public availability before the May 8 deadline is proven: PX-2 (SDNY order, May 6, 2026) directs docketing/unsealing of the Note, and PX-3 (AP via PBS, published May 6, updated May 7) reports it was made public. But the rule requires the note be 'credibly reported to have been written by Jeffrey Epstein.' The strongest sources do not credibly report Epstein authorship; they report only a purported/alleged attribution and affirmatively flag it as uncertain. PX-3 states 'It is unclear who wrote the note' and that it was absent from the government death reports, DOJ file release, and the 128-page IG report. PX-2 is an access order using 'alleged'/'purportedly authored,' not an authorship finding. Provenance runs solely through Tartaglione (a convicted quadruple murderer and former cellmate) via his own lawyers in an unrelated case, and D-1 adds a direct forgery challenge from Mark Epstein. Plaintiff's counterweights (2021 sealing predating the 2026 emails; attempts to authenticate in 2019-2020) show authorship is possible, not more likely than not; attempts to authenticate are not successful authentication. Credible reporting of a purported attribution is not credible reporting that Epstein wrote it. On preponderance, availability is shown but credible Epstein authorship is not, so the proposition is not demonstrated.

While the evidence establishes public availability of a purported suicide note by the deadline, it does not meet the preponderance standard for credible reporting of Epstein's authorship. The admitted exhibits consistently use qualifiers like 'purported' and 'alleged,' note unclear authorship, lack official validation, and show disputed provenance through Tartaglione. Under the market rule, both credible authorship reporting AND intent as a suicide/final message must be shown, and the record remains balanced on these elements.

The admission record shows public availability was established before the deadline, but authorship remains speculative. Key exhibits explicitly state 'it is unclear who wrote the note,' and include Mark Epstein's forgery claim and absence from DOJ files. While intent is plausible from content, preponderance requires more credible authorship evidence than 'alleged'/'purported' reports. The rule requires 'credibly reported to have been written by Epstein' — a standard not met when major evidence labels authorship unclear.

The admitted record contains no credible primary statement from Clavicular confirming a pregnancy; the only supporting fragment from Forbes is incomplete and conflicting secondary reports (Times of India, Hindustan Times, Express Tribune) indicate no verification. Under the preponderance standard, the balance of evidence favors that no qualified announcement was made.

The proposition requires a credible announcement by Clavicular that he and a partner were expecting a baby through pregnancy; the rule excludes jokes/non-credible claims and allows media only as a 'definitive consensus.' The admitted record does not meet preponderance. The only sources preserving actual quoted words (PX-8/D-4 Times of India, D-5 Hindustan Times, D-6 Express Tribune) describe non-qualifying material: future-oriented remarks ('I will be a dad in the next few months') and a proposed pregnancy contest, with TOI expressly finding no proof, no confirmed girlfriend, no medical update, no video, and no Clavicular statement confirming pregnancy, and calling the viral claim false. The pro-plaintiff evidence is thin: the final Yes state (PX-7) proves platform resolution, not the factual predicate, and the same Forbes extraction (PX-6) notes the market description and platform clarification said No while price said Yes, so the disputed oracle outcome is not independent proof. Forbes's isolated fragment 'gotten a girl pregnant' is a partial metered-page snippet lacking the surrounding sentence, speaker context, source chain, and credibility basis, and cannot be distinguished from the very rumor the fuller sources debunk. Rather than a definitive media consensus, the record shows conflicting reporting plus admitted gaps (PX-3/PX-5: no VOD, transcript, representative statement, clarification, or UMA records). The more likely conclusion is provocative contest/aspiration remarks and an unconfirmed viral rumor, not a credible announcement of an existing partner pregnancy. Proposition not demonstrated.

The preponderance of evidence supports the proposition. The official Polymarket resolution to 'Yes' under a rule requiring credible announcements is probative evidence. Forbes reporting specifically cites a pregnancy announcement by Clavicular that matches the rule requirements ('gotten a girl pregnant'). While conflicting reports exist and original source material is lacking, the rules explicitly permit resolution based on credible media reporting. The market outcome and Forbes characterization together meet the preponderance standard.

The Forbes reporting characterizes the market dispute as being over a streamer who told his audience 'he had gotten a girl pregnant,' aligning with the rule's requirement for a credible pregnancy announcement. This is supported by Polymarket's final Yes resolution with 99.9% consensus. While there are conflicting secondary reports about contest proposals, the market-specific evidence and platform resolution meet the preponderance standard for the proposition.

The admitted evidence preserves the central livestream statements and demonstrates they were future-oriented intent ("I must have children. I must get children today. I will be a dad in the next few months, guys. I am telling you."), not a present-tense announcement of an existing partner pregnancy. Times of India, Hindustan Times, and Express Tribune all quote these exact words and frame them as a proposed pregnancy contest/out-of-context remarks. The viral claim about a girlfriend pregnancy after 10 days was explicitly debunked (no proof, no confirmed girlfriend, no medical update, no confirmation from Clavicular). The Forbes fragment mentioning "gotten a girl pregnant" is incomplete, partial, and lacks source chain; the same Forbes piece notes the market description and platform clarification said No. Under the rule requiring credible announcements and excluding jokes/non-credible claims, the plaintiff has not carried the preponderance burden. The best-supported interpretation of the admitted record is that Clavicular made provocative remarks about wanting children and a pregnancy contest, but not a credible announcement that he and a partner were expecting a baby through pregnancy.

The admitted record preserves only the market rule, a disputed final Yes state, and secondary/social descriptions; no primary Clavicular statement, clip, or transcript exists, and every admitted description of what he said calls it 'claimed/joked,' 'vague,' 'no proof,' or 'likely a viral joke,' with contest/future-fatherhood remarks not identifying an existing partner pregnancy.
