The admitted record contains no signed bilateral agreement, no matching official confirmations, and no permanent-cessation language by June 15, 2025; instead, official U.S. and Iranian sources and credible reporting show cancelled talks, refusal to deal, and later hostilities.

The admitted evidence contains no signed agreement or bilateral public confirmation of a permanent peace deal by the deadline; instead it shows cancelled talks, continued hostilities, and official statements refuting any such deal. The admitted record lacks evidence of a written agreement signed/adopted by both governments or matched public confirmation establishing a permanent peace deal ending military hostilities by the deadline. Exhibits show only negotiations, conditional statements, and cancellation of talks.

No evidence shows a signed agreement or bilateral confirmation of permanent peace by June 15, 2025. Evidence shows only conditional talks, cancellation of negotiations, and later statements of refusal to reach a deal.

D-6 describes June 15 as a planned meeting to 'craft' an agreement and D-7 states Iran refused to reach a deal, and no exhibit contains language ending or permanently ceasing U.S.-Iran military hostilities by the deadline. No evidence of bilateral affirmation of permanent peace deal. White House fact sheet confirms Iran refused to reach deal in June.

The admitted evidence fails to demonstrate by a preponderance that Iran and the US agreed to a permanent peace deal by June 15, 2025. Samantha's authenticated confession proves she falsely claimed to have read the required Stephenson essay, providing the false foundation for her explicit deadline assurances. Peter reasonably relied on these assurances, confirmed twice, before committing to substantial commercial printing costs.

The record shows Samantha gave a specific deadline assurance, Peter relied on it, and her authenticated confession proves the factual basis for that assurance was false, satisfying the preponderance standard.

PX-1/DX-1 show Samantha twice assured completion by the handoff, expressly grounding that assurance on false present-status claims ('I have read Stephenson,' 'already in drafting and revision'), and the authenticated PX-4 confession proves the reading claim false, making the timing assurance materially misleading when made. Peter's reliance was reasonable: he set the go/no-go condition, re-confirmed before approving the invoice (PX-2), and relied on the retained writer with superior knowledge of her own preparation. On the record this linked inference is more likely than the defense's narrower reading, satisfying the proposition by a preponderance.
Samantha materially misrepresented completion readiness through false claims of prerequisite work, and Peter reasonably relied on these representations when committing to commercial expenses.

Samantha misrepresented her preparation but the record lacks proof that this false statement made her completion deadline assurance materially misleading. The evidence shows she was actively drafting and revising when she made the assurances, and it does not prove the package was late or she knew it couldn't be completed by the deadline.

The record shows Samantha affirmed she had read the essay and guaranteed timely delivery, then admitted she had not read it, making her timing promise materially false, and Peter relied on that assurance before approving costly rush production.

The admitted thread shows Samantha's completion assurance was expressly grounded in her claim that she had read Stephenson and was 'already in drafting and revision,' yet the confession admits she had not read the full essay before the first draft and implied she had, making the timing representation materially misleading. Timing was explicitly material because Peter tied his rush-print go/no-go to it. Peter disclosed the decision, obtained a clear assurance, and reconfirmed before approving costs, establishing reasonable reliance; the defense's missing-records points bear on damages, not on these liability elements, so the proposition is more likely true than not.

Samantha's bundled false claim about foundational work completion materially misled the deadline assurance. Peter's reliance was reasonable given explicit disclosure of consequences and reconfirmation before commitment.

Samantha's timing assurance was materially linked to her false claim of having read Stephenson, which she later admitted was untrue. Peter's dual confirmations before financial commitments show reasonable reliance. The record proves by preponderance that the timing representation was false and relied upon.

Samantha's admission that she had not read Stephenson before drafting, paired with her assurance that she had read it and could complete the package on time, makes the completion representation materially misleading. Peter reasonably relied by disclosing the go/no-go decision, obtaining explicit assurances, and reconfirming before approving the rush print setup.

PX-2 shows Samantha gave an explicit completion-timing assurance ('complete in time for tonight's handoff') bundled with a present-tense claim that she had read Stephenson, which her authenticated confession (PX-4/PX-6) admits was false, making the timing assurance a materially misleading half-truth about the source-grounded package required by PX-1. Peter disclosed the go/no-go consequence, asked twice, and acted on the confirmation (PX-3, PX-7), which is reasonable reliance not requiring a forensic audit. The damages and time-and-token logs corroborate that the engagement in fact failed and required post-admission cleanup, so on a preponderance both elements are demonstrated.
Samantha's false representation ...

The record shows Samantha's assurance about reading Stephenson before drafting is false, but doesn't prove she couldn't complete by handoff after revision. The session summary indicates active refinement and revision, and missing records on final handoff status prevent preponderance determination.

The confession proves Samantha falsely claimed to have read Stephenson before drafting, a prerequisite for the assigned source-grounded deliverable. Her March 10-11 completion assurances were materially misleading because they rested on this false premise. Peter reasonably relied by conditioning costly print commitments on her explicit timing confirmations and then acting as warned.
The confession shows Samantha falsely claimed to have read the source, making her deadline assurance misleading, and Peter’s approval note confirms his reasonable reliance on that assurance.

The preponderance of evidence shows Trump said 'Ronaldo' in press-gaggle material explicitly distinguished from the FIFA event in the source transcripts. The market rules specify resolution based on draw video/transcripts, which don't include these pre-event remarks. The proposition to expand coverage to pre-program venue remarks is not supported by governing rules.

The admitted evidence shows Trump said 'Ronaldo' in a press-gaggle explicitly labeled as 'before the FIFA event.' The market rules state 'Video of the FIFA World Cup Draw will be primarily used to resolve the market' and the formal draw transcript contains no Ronaldo. There is no evidence the market rules include pre-event red-carpet remarks. The resolution sources do not include the media channels (TV Azteca, Telemundo, etc.) that captured the remarks. Therefore, the strike does not meet the resolution criteria.

The admitted record proves Trump said 'Ronaldo' in a press-gaggle/red-carpet transcript explicitly marked as occurring before the FIFA event and compiled from multiple external media sources (DX-3). The official Kalshi market rules state that video of the FIFA World Cup Draw is primary and transcripts of the FIFA World Cup Draw are fallback sources (DX-1). The formal FIFA World Cup Drawing transcript from Roll Call/Factbase contains no 'Ronaldo' match (DX-2). Plaintiff has not provided any admitted evidence that the market's governing rules include pre-event red-carpet press coverage within the 'FIFA World Cup Draw' resolution window. Without admitted evidence of such inclusion, preponderance favors the challenged No resolution.

The record shows Trump said "Ronaldo" in a press‑gaggle transcript captured at the venue immediately before the formal draw, and Kalshi rules require the phrase to be said "as part of FIFA World Cup Draw" with video primary and transcripts fallback. The rules do not exclude pre‑event, press‑covered remarks, and the event was opened to press, satisfying the live‑stream condition. Thus the pre‑event remarks count, meeting the preponderance burden for Yes.

On preponderance, the admitted record does not show the pre-event red-carpet remarks fall within the market's covered window. The official Kalshi rule capture states resolution uses 'Video of the FIFA World Cup Draw' primarily and 'transcripts of the FIFA World Cup Draw' as fallback, requiring the word be said 'as part of FIFA World Cup Draw.' The only source containing 'Ronaldo' is a press-gaggle/red-carpet transcript whose own note states the material occurred '18 minutes before the FIFA event,' engaged the press pool ~3 minutes, and was pieced together from TV Azteca, Telemundo, SBT Media, El Destape and other non-official feeds; Trump's remarks reference meeting Ronaldo 'at the White House,' consistent with pre-program chatter. The formal draw transcript (Roll Call/Factbase and Rev) contains no 'Ronaldo' match. Plaintiff's evidence (White House gallery, Bernard ledger) proves venue proximity and that this was a version/timeframe dispute (result recorded N), but not that the pre-program remarks are within the covered draw video/transcript. The press/live-stream clause is a qualifying condition, not an inclusion rule sweeping in all arrival feeds. Plaintiff bears the burden to expand 'during the draw' to include immediately-prior remarks and has not carried it; the challenged No is better supported.

Trump's red carpet remarks immediately before the formal program were part of the public-facing, livestreamed coverage of the FIFA World Cup Draw event. Kalshi rules show video is primary resolution method, and 'during' includes livestreamed red carpet coverage.

The admitted record establishes Trump said 'Ronaldo' in public remarks at the venue, but repeatedly describes those remarks as occurring immediately before the formal draw program began or prior to the start of the programme (PX-1, PX-3, PX-5/DX-S3). Under ordinary language, 'during' an event is distinct from 'before' it began. The Kalshi rule requires the word be said 'as part of FIFA World Cup Draw' (PX-4/DX-S2). The only Ronaldo-specific external source (PX-5/DX-S3) reports Kalshi resolved No based on Version/Timeframe, corroborating the timing boundary. Plaintiff has not shown, by a preponderance, that pre-program red-carpet remarks were within the market-covered draw event rather than adjacent to it. The Yes resolution is not demonstrated.

Admitted record includes direct pre‑event remarks attributing two Ronaldo statements to Trump (PX‑3) and market rules indicating video of the draw is primary (PX‑4) with no rule limiting resolution to the formal on‑stage transcript. The secondary dispute index reporting a prior No result is secondary and does not outweigh the primary evidence. Preponderance of the evidence therefore supports that Trump’s remarks at the venue immediately before the formal program count as occurring during the draw, so the strike resolves Yes.

Under preponderance, the proposition is not demonstrated. The proposition requires that remarks made immediately before the formal program count as occurring 'during the FIFA World Cup Draw.' The admitted record consistently places the Ronaldo remarks outside that boundary: the packet (PX-1/DX-1) frames them as 'immediately before the formal draw program began,' and the only Ronaldo-specific source (PX-5/DX-4) reports the strike was said 'to the press on the red carpet prior to the start of the programme,' with the dispute labeled 'Version, Timeframe' and 'Result: N.' The Kalshi rule extraction (PX-4/DX-3) is a Canada-strike page requiring the word be said 'as part of FIFA World Cup Draw' with video 'of the FIFA World Cup Draw' primary; its press-open/live-stream clause is a no-resolution condition, not an expansion of the covered window to red-carpet or pre-show content. DX-5 confirms only parent-event metadata (markets empty) and supplies no Ronaldo-specific rule broadening the scope. PX-3 tends to show an utterance occurred, but plaintiff bears the burden to prove that a pre-program remark falls within the market-covered event, and the record's own timing descriptions ('immediately before,' 'prior to the start') plus the sole Ronaldo-specific reported outcome cut against inclusion. The evidence does not make it more likely than not that the utterance occurred during the covered draw.

The record shows Trump mentioned Ronaldo before the formal program, but lacks sufficient evidence that this falls within the market's 'during the draw' requirement. The secondary source reporting the market resolved as No due to timeframe issues, combined with the absence of primary video evidence confirming event coverage, means plaintiff has not met the preponderance standard.

The admitted evidence shows the Ronaldo remarks occurred before the FIFA event and prior to the programme (PX-1, PX-3), the formal transcript lacks the word, and the market's reported result was No under Timeframe rules, so the proposition is not demonstrated.

The admitted record shows Trump said Ronaldo only in red‑carpet remarks before the formal draw and the Bernard ledger confirms the market applied Version/Timeframe rules to reach a No result; no admitted rule expands the draw scope, so the proposition is not demonstrated by preponderance.

The record shows Trump said "Ronaldo," but every admitted timing fact places the remarks outside the draw: the Senate transcript notes he spoke on the red carpet "before the FIFA event," the Bernard ledger records the exact market as decided N under Timeframe because the words came "prior to the start of the programme," and the formal program transcript apparently lacks the strike. Plaintiff bears the burden to prove the remarks occurred "during" the draw and offers no admitted rule broadening the timeframe, so the proposition is not proven by a preponderance.

The record proves Trump said 'Ronaldo' pre-event but fails to demonstrate these remarks occurred during the market's qualifying timeframe. The Bernard Bulletin ledger reports resolution under Version/Timeframe rules resulting in No, and no official Kalshi rule expanding the timeframe was admitted.

The admitted record shows Trump's remarks were documented as 'before the FIFA event' and 'prior to the start of the programme'. The Bernard Bulletin ledger reports the result as 'N' for 'Version, Timeframe' rules. No official market rule evidence was admitted that would expand the timeframe to include the red-carpet remarks.

The record contains an official, public transcript (PX‑5) showing Trump said the standalone word "Ronaldo" during remarks at the John F. Kennedy Center linked to the FIFA World Cup Draw, and the mention‑market terms do not limit "during the FIFA World Cup Draw" to the formal program only. No admitted evidence excludes these pre‑program remarks, so by a preponderance of the evidence the strike satisfies the contract. Hence the proposition is demonstrated.

The record proves Trump said the standalone word Ronaldo, but only in an official transcript titled an exchange with reporters 'prior to' the FIFA World Cup Draw, while the separate admitted formal drawing transcript ('Attends the FIFA World Cup Drawing') omits Ronaldo. The terms require the word to be said 'during' the specified event time period, and the ordinary reading of 'during the FIFA World Cup Draw' is the draw program, not a separately titled pre-event press exchange. Plaintiff supplies no market term, source hierarchy, or program metadata bridging the pre-event remarks into the event period, so the preponderance burden that the mention occurred during the market event is not met.

The preponderance of evidence shows the market contract specifically covered remarks made during the FIFA World Cup Draw program. While Trump undoubtedly mentioned 'Ronaldo' in official, event-linked remarks immediately preceding the Draw, the market language, official transcripts, and separate formal program recording demonstrate these remarks occurred prior to the event period. The defense successfully preserved the critical distinction between event-adjacent remarks and the event itself as referenced in the market question.

Market titled "What will Trump say during the FIFA World Cup Draw?" without narrow exclusions. Offical public remarks at event venue with FIFA president present were tied to Draw context. Terms allow official context events; formal draw transcript includes ceremony before ball selection, confirming "during" includes preamble. Preponderance standard met for Yes.
The record proves Trump said Ronaldo but in remarks explicitly documented as Prior to the Draw, not during. The market asked about during the FIFA World Cup Draw. Plaintiff has not met preponderance.

That Trump said the standalone word "Ronaldo" is undisputed, but the decisive element is whether the market's "during the FIFA World Cup Draw" period covered the reporter exchange, whose own official source title describes it as remarks "Prior to" the draw and a separate artifact from the formal program (Rev), which lacked "Ronaldo." The governing terms make event scope a real condition (included only if part of the official presentation/period), and plaintiff supplied no market rule, source designation, or official-presentation evidence placing that pre-program exchange inside the covered period. Absence of an express exclusion is not proof of inclusion, so the challenger has not met the preponderance burden to overturn the No resolution.

While Trump explicitly mentioned 'Ronaldo' in the pre-event reporter exchange, the evidence shows this was expressly labeled by its source as occurring prior to the formal FIFA World Cup Draw program. The market language ('during') and terms document do not clearly extend coverage to pre-event exchanges. The formal program transcript contained no reference to Ronaldo. Without express evidence that this market included pre-program exchanges, the record doesn't support overturning the resolution.

The admitted record shows Trump said 'Ronaldo' in a public, official exchange immediately before the formal draw program at the Kennedy Center, tied to the 2026 World Cup Draw Event. The presmention terms require the word during the specified time period but do not narrow 'during the FIFA World Cup Draw' to only the formal stage segment. Kalshi's market terms exclude rehearsals and unofficial recordings, but this is an official public transcript with FIFA President present. No market-specific exclusion was provided—the No resolution lacks evidence-based justification.

Under preponderance: Trump said standalone 'Ronaldo' multiple times in an official public reporter exchange at the Kennedy Center (the draw venue) on Dec 5, 2025, tied throughout by headings to the '2026 World Cup Draw Event' with FIFA President Infantino present (ev_9cd6fa5297f3_pre-event-reporter-exchange, ev_c7c5425b23cf). The market asked 'during the FIFA World Cup Draw,' not 'during the formal stage transcript.' The governing PRESMENTION/NEWMENTION terms (ev_a373371a8c9c) allow event-based periods and exclude only specified categories (rehearsals, sound checks, unofficial recordings) that this exchange does not fit. Defendant's formal-program comparator (ev_177c67332111) lacks 'Ronaldo,' but proves only that the formal segment differed; it does not show the market excluded pre-program venue remarks. No admitted rule narrows the scope to the formal program. The natural reading of the market title, supported by the terms and the public, official, event-linked nature of the remarks, includes them. It is therefore more likely than not the strike should resolve Yes.

The admitted record shows Trump said the standalone word "Ronaldo" in a public, official reporter exchange at the Kennedy Center that was event‑linked to the 2026 FIFA World Cup Draw. The governing terms do not exclude such pre‑program, venue‑based remarks, and no admitted market rule limits the period to the formal stage only. By a preponderance of the evidence, it is more likely than not that the remark falls within the market’s "during the FIFA World Cup Draw" period, satisfying the proposition. Hence the strike should resolve Yes.

While Trump clearly said 'Ronaldo' in pre-event remarks, the plaintiff hasn't proven by preponderance that Kalshi's market rules included such remarks within the resolving event. The verification hierarchy specifying 'video/transcripts of FIFA World Cup Draw' creates reasonable doubt about whether pre-event remarks qualified under 'as part of' the draw.

The admitted record shows Trump said 'Ronaldo' in a public exchange explicitly titled 'prior to' the FIFA World Cup Draw (PX-2), but the formal draw transcript (PX-6) contains no Ronaldo. Market terms require words spoken 'during' the event, and without explicit market language including pre-event remarks at the venue, the preponderance standard for resolving 'Yes' is not met.

The market language 'as part of FIFA World Cup Draw' is broader than a formal-program-only reading. PX-2 proves Trump said 'Ronaldo' in official White House-released remarks at the draw venue with FIFA President Infantino present. PX-5 includes The White House as a source agency, and PX-3's NEWMENTION terms do not exclude public, source-published, event-linked remarks. The pre-event transcript is not backstage, rehearsal, or unofficial material. Plaintiff's reading requires fewer unstated additions. Under preponderance, the strike should resolve Yes.

The record shows Trump said "Ronaldo" in a pre‑event reporter exchange, but there is no evidence that this exchange is part of the official FIFA World Cup Draw event for which the market contracts define the payout period. The formal draw transcript, the primary source under the market terms, contains no Ronaldo. The governing terms are ambiguous and do not explicitly include pre‑program remarks. Under the preponderance standard, the plaintiff has not shown that the word was spoken during the market‑defined event; thus the proposition is not demonstrated.

The admitted Kalshi rule (PX-5) resolves this market from the FIFA World Cup Draw itself, using video of the Draw primarily and transcripts of the Draw as fallback, yet the only evidence containing 'Ronaldo' (PX-2) is expressly titled remarks 'prior to' the Draw, while the formal Draw transcript (PX-6) returned no match for Ronaldo. Plaintiff proved only a related, pre-event reporter exchange and never bridged it into the Draw's resolving source set, and the terms (PX-3) require the word during the covered event, so non-exclusion is not inclusion. Under preponderance the challenger bears the burden, and this record leaves the boundary question unproven rather than more-likely-than-not for Yes.

The GovInfo transcript explicitly states remarks were 'Prior to the 2026 FIFA World Cup Draw', and U.S. Soccer schedule confirms the draw began at 12 p.m. ET, with pre-draw coverage at 11:30. The 11:13 a.m. exchange was outside the market's timeframe. Market term 'during' refers to the draw event itself, not prior activities; no evidence of inclusion in the terms.

The admitted evidence proves Trump said 'Ronaldo' as a standalone word at 11:13 a.m. during a reporter exchange at the Kennedy Center, but this exchange is explicitly titled 'Prior to the 2026 FIFA World Cup Draw.' DX-1 establishes the Final Draw was scheduled for 12:00-2:00 p.m. ET. The plain meaning of 'during the FIFA World Cup Draw' requires the draw event to be underway. Since the utterance occurred before the formal draw window, it does not fall within the market's specified time period. The governing terms do not expand the event period to include pre-program reporter remarks. Plaintiff has shown proximity and event linkage, not inclusion. On a preponderance standard, the time-period element is not satisfied.

The record proves Trump said "Ronaldo" aloud, but the GovInfo transcript shows the remarks occurred at 11:13 a.m., prior to the scheduled FIFA World Cup Draw which began at 12 p.m. ET (as shown by the defendant’s U.S. Soccer schedule evidence). The governing terms do not expressly include pre‑program public remarks, and the ordinary meaning of "during the FIFA World Cup Draw" refers to the draw event itself. Thus, the preponderance of the evidence does not support that the strike resolves Yes.

The utterance is proven: the official GovInfo transcript records Trump saying the standalone word 'Ronaldo' twice ('I got to meet Ronaldo the other day' and 'But Ronaldo is great'), not bracketed inaudible, satisfying the distinct-word rule in the PRESMENTION/NEWMENTION terms. The proposition fails on the time-period element. The market (PX-1) asks what Trump said 'during the FIFA World Cup Draw.' Plaintiff's own primary source is titled 'Remarks in an Exchange With Reporters Prior to the 2026 FIFA World Cup Draw,' with metadata placing the exchange at 11:13 a.m. as an exchange with reporters. Defendant's U.S. Soccer exhibit places the Final Draw at 12 p.m. ET, pre-draw coverage at 11:30 a.m. ET, and the draw window at 12-2 p.m. ET, so the 11:13 exchange occurred before even the pre-draw coverage began. The governing terms permit event-based periods and state the Exchange may specify what an event includes, but no admitted market text specifies that pre-program reporter remarks count; the exclusions for backstage/rehearsals/captions are limiting rules, not an affirmative inclusion of all non-excluded venue remarks. Under the preponderance standard, plaintiff proved venue and topical proximity but did not prove inclusion within the draw's time period; 'during' ordinarily requires the event to be underway. The challenged No resolution stands; the proposition is not demonstrated.

The preponderance of evidence shows the 'Ronaldo' utterance occurred prior to the FIFA World Cup Draw event period. The market terms did not explicitly include pre-program remarks, and the transcript metadata/timing evidence confirms the remarks occurred before both the pre-draw coverage and formal draw window.

The admitted record proves Trump said 'Ronaldo' at 11:13 a.m. at the Kennedy Center (DX-1), but DX-1's official title explicitly labels these as 'Remarks in an Exchange With Reporters Prior to the 2026 FIFA World Cup Draw' and DX-2 establishes the Draw commenced at 12:00. Under a preponderance standard, the best evidence shows the utterance occurred before the countable 'during' period, not within it. The market's language and governing terms require the word to be said during the specified time period; the record more likely than not places these remarks outside that window.

Official GovInfo transcript labels Trump’s Ronaldo remarks as an exchange with reporters "Prior to the FIFA World Cup Draw" at 11:13 a.m., while the FIFA official page sets the draw start at 12:00 p.m.; thus the utterance occurred outside the countable event period. The market terms do not expressly include pre‑program remarks, so the record does not meet the preponderance burden to demonstrate the strike should resolve Yes.

Trump did say "Ronaldo," but the official GovInfo transcript (DX-1) titles those remarks an exchange with reporters "Prior to" the FIFA World Cup Draw and notes he spoke at 11:13 a.m., while FIFA's official page (DX-2) fixes the Draw's commencement at 12:00 local time, placing the utterance before the countable event period. The governing terms require the word to be said during the specified time period and do not deem pre-event press remarks included by default, so treating an unexcluded pre-program remark as automatically inside the Draw would reverse the preponderance burden that rests on Yes. The plaintiff's weaker exhibits (a White House page lacking the video and nonofficial YouTube metadata) do not overcome the official title and timing, so the proposition is not demonstrated.

The preponderance of evidence establishes the 'Ronaldo' remarks occurred at 11:13 AM prior to the FIFA World Cup Draw's official 12:00 PM commencement. The market's temporal limitation ('during') and governing terms don't include pre-event venue remarks, and plaintiff provided no market-specific rules to expand this definition.

Official GovInfo transcript titled "Remarks in an Exchange With Reporters Prior to the 2026 FIFA World Cup Draw" and FIFA Final Draw page confirm Trump spoke at 11:13 AM before the 12:00 Start time. Evidence shows remarks occurred prior to the event, not during it. Rationale must be grounded in evidence: plaintiff failed to show the word was spoken during the specified time period.

The record shows Trump said "Ronaldo" only in a pre‑program reporter exchange labeled "Prior to the draw", and the market’s source hierarchy prioritizes the official draw video/transcript, which contains no such utterance; thus the proposition is not more likely true than false.

The record proves Trump said "Ronaldo" only in a GovInfo source self-titled and categorized as a reporter exchange "Prior to" the draw, and the transcript's own markers ("a little late to get in there," "They wait") place it before entry into the program, while the more specific Kalshi source rule points to video/transcripts of the draw itself. The formal draw transcript contains no "Ronaldo" match, so plaintiff has not shown by a preponderance that a qualifying utterance occurred "during" or "as part of" the FIFA World Cup Draw.

The preponderance of evidence supports that Trump's public remarks at the venue immediately before the formal program, containing the standalone word "Ronaldo," occurred as part of the World Cup Draw event context. While labeled "prior," they were official, event-linked, and occurred at the venue with the program waiting to begin. The market's broad phrasing ("as part of") and absence of explicit formal-program limitations support inclusion under the contractual terms.

TheGovInfo transcript explicitly titled 'Remarks in an Exchange With Reporters Prior to the 2026 FIFA World Cup Draw' confirms the utterance occurred before the formal draw ceremony. While the remarks were event-adjacent, 'during' in common usage refers to the formal ceremony itself. The market's 'video of the FIFA World Cup Draw' source rule and the lack of Exchange specification extending 'during' to pre-ceremony remarks mean the evidence does not meet the preponderance standard for Yes resolution.

The admitted GovInfo transcript (PX-3) proves Trump said 'Ronaldo' in remarks explicitly titled 'Prior to the 2026 FIFA World Cup Draw,' with internal timing showing he was outside waiting to enter while people inside waited. The formal FIFA World Cup Drawing transcript (DX-1) contains no Ronaldo. The market asked what Trump would say 'during' the draw, and the Kalshi page extract (PX-4) points to video/transcripts of the FIFA World Cup Draw as primary sources. 'Prior to' is not 'during.' Under a preponderance standard, the proposition is not demonstrated.

Admitted reporting (Times of Israel, AP) and even the defendant's CRS exhibit show that during the window (Dec. 8-17, 2024) Israel captured and seized control of positions inside Syrian territory—the Syrian side of Mount Hermon and the UNDOF buffer zone, distinct from the excluded Golan—for the first time since 1974. Israeli officials (PMO, Netanyahu, Katz) ordered the takeover and announced forces would remain until an arrangement ensured Israel's security, which is intent to establish control. The 'temporary/defensive' framing limits duration and motive but does not negate a proactive armed takeover of Syrian territory, so the proposition is proven by a preponderance.

While Israeli forces established temporary control in Syrian territory, the preponderance of evidence shows defensive intent rather than offensive intent to establish control as required by the market rules. Official statements consistently characterize the operation as temporary and defensive.

Evidence confirms Israeli forces captured Syrian territory on Mount Hermon (outside Golan Heights), entered buffer zone established by 1974 Israel-Syria Disengagement Agreement, and announced intent to remain until security arranged. The market's "any portion" standard is met despite temporary characterization—the seizure of control and sustained presence constitutes established military control. Official sources (Israel, UN) describe this as occupation and breach of agreement.

The admitted evidence demonstrates by a preponderance that between Dec 8-17, 2024, Israeli forces entered the UNDOF buffer zone and Syrian side of Mount Hermon (proven Syrian territory, not Golan), captured strategic positions, and Netanyahu announced Israel would remain until a security arrangement ensured Israel's security - satisfying the elements of a military offensive intended to establish control over a portion of Syria.

The admitted evidence shows Israel’s IDF captured the Syrian side of Mount Hermon and the UNDOF buffer zone on Dec 8‑9 2024, seized positions, and issued official statements (PMO Dec 17) that forces would remain there until a security arrangement was achieved. This satisfies the market’s elements of a military offensive, entry into Syrian territory, and intent to establish control over a portion of Syria within the September‑December 2024 window, meeting the preponderance standard.

The admitted record proves temporary Israeli control of Syrian buffer zones but lacks preponderant evidence this constituted a military offensive intended to establish control. Official Israeli statements consistently characterize the action as defensive and temporary, with intent conditional on security arrangements rather than sovereign control.

The record shows Israel's military took control of Syrian territory (Mount Hermon buffer zone) and established deployment intended to remain until security conditions improved. Official Israeli sources ordered "take over" and "take control" of Syrian territory, UNDOF confirmed it violated the 1974 Agreement, and AP reporting described "seizing control" of buffer zone in Syrian territory. The proposition requires only "any portion of Syria" with "intent to establish control," which the record establishes despite being framed as temporary/defensive.

The admitted record establishes by a preponderance that Israel commenced a military offensive intended to establish control over Syrian territory by December 31, 2024. Official Israeli statements confirm orders to 'take over these positions' (PX-2) and to 'temporarily take control of the buffer zone in Syria' (PX-3), with continued deployment on Mount Hermon until a security arrangement was found (PX-5). UNDOF's press statement (PX-4) confirms significant IDF movements within the area of separation and that IDF forces remained in multiple locations there, constituting a violation of the 1974 Disengagement Agreement. Credible reporting (PX-6, PX-7) corroborates that the IDF took over the Syrian side of Mount Hermon and that Netanyahu visited the Syrian side, stating that Israeli troops would remain until a security arrangement was in place. PX-1's resolution rationale notes that the Syrian peak of Mount Hermon is not part of the Golan Heights carveout, satisfying the geographic element. The defense's AP exhibit (D-2) repeats the same control language while framing the move as temporary and defensive, but the proposition does not require permanent control or exclude defensive motives. The words 'take over,' 'take control,' 'remain,' and 'continued deployment' combined with UNDOF's confirmation and credible reporting meet the burden of showing an intent to establish control over Syrian territory.

Israel’s official statements (PX‑2, PX‑3, PX‑5) order the IDF to take over and temporarily control Syrian positions, and to remain until a security arrangement is found, while UNDOF confirms IDF presence in the Syrian‑side area of separation (PX‑4) and media reports (PX‑6, PX‑7) corroborate control of the Syrian peak of Mount Hermon. This satisfies the market’s trigger of a military offensive intended to establish control over any portion of Syria, meeting the preponderance standard.

Israel's own official statements confirm orders to take over positions and 'temporarily take control of the buffer zone in Syria' (PX-2, PX-3), with continued IDF deployment on the Syrian side of Mount Hermon 'until another arrangement is found' (PX-5), corroborated by UNDOF's confirmation of IDF movement and presence in the area of separation (PX-4) and credible reporting placing the seizure on the Syrian side of Mount Hermon, which PX-1 says is outside the Golan carveout (PX-6, PX-7, PX-R1). The 'temporary/defensive' framing goes to duration and motive, not the market's element of intent to establish control over any portion of Syria, which is satisfied. On a preponderance standard the proposition is more likely true than not.

Israeli forces entered Syrian territory during December 2024, occupied the Syrian side of Mount Hermon (outside Golan Heights), established military positions and fortifications, and publicly stated intent to remain until security conditions were met. UN confirmed violation of 1974 disengagement agreement. This meets preponderance standard for control over Syrian territory.

The admitted record establishes by a preponderance that in December 2024 Israeli forces entered Syrian territory on the Syrian side of Mount Hermon and the UN buffer zone, seized and held positions, fortified them, and declared intent to remain until a security arrangement was found. PX-1 and PX-2 (AP) fix timing and geography; PX-2 quotes Netanyahu's intent to stay and Katz's order to fortify. PX-3 (UNSC) and PX-4 (UN) confirm IDF positions in the area of separation. DX-1 concedes troops in the zone and takeover of a Syrian post. Seizing and holding Syrian territory to meet Israel's security conditions satisfies the market definition of a military offensive intended to establish control. The Golan carve-out does not apply to Mount Hermon's Syrian side. Official and credible sources corroborate commencement within the window and control. Under the preponderance standard, the proposition is demonstrated.

The admitted record shows Israeli forces entered Syrian territory (Syrian side of Mount Hermon) in December 2024, seized positions, established a buffer‑zone, and, per official statements and UN confirmations, intended to remain until security arrangements were secured, satisfying the market’s requirement of a military offensive intended to establish control over a portion of Syria.

By a preponderance, the admitted record proves each element. Timing: AP (ev-01) and Reuters/Arab News (ev-05) fix Israeli forces moving into the Syrian buffer zone in December 2024, with Netanyahu's Dec. 17 visit inside the window. Geography outside the market-excluded Golan: AP places Netanyahu on the Mount Hermon summit 'inside Syria, ~10km from the Israel-held Golan Heights,' and Reuters confirms troops entered the demilitarized zone inside Syria including the Syrian side of Mount Hermon, taking over an abandoned Syrian military post; PX-0 confirms the Syrian peak is not part of the Golan. Conduct: forces crossed in, captured/seized a ~400 sq km area, took a Syrian post, and Katz ordered a 'presence, including fortifications' for a possible extended stay; UN transcript S/PV.9857 (ev-02) officially confirms the IDF 'established multiple positions in the area of separation,' with Syria and P5 members (France, China) confirming crossings and seizures. Intent to establish control: Netanyahu said forces would stay 'until another arrangement is found that ensures Israel's security,' and Saar described 'targeted and temporary control.' The defense's temporary/defensive/no-permanent-presence framing explains motive but does not negate the seizure and holding of Syrian ground; a temporary military seizure held until security conditions are met satisfies 'a military offensive intended to establish control over any portion of Syria.' Official confirmation by the UN and P5 members is present. The proposition is demonstrated.

The admitted evidence establishes by a preponderance that Israel commenced a military offensive intended to establish control over Syrian territory. Israeli forces entered the Syrian side of Mount Hermon, took and fortified positions, and announced continued control until security conditions were met - all within the December 2024 market window. This satisfies the proposition despite defensive framing.

The admitted record proves by a preponderance that between Nov 3, 2025 and Jan 31, 2026, U.S. forces commenced a military offensive (Operation Absolute Resolve) in Caracas using coordinated air, ground, and maritime power against a fortified compound and Venezuelan threats (PX-1, PX-2, PX-3). This created a protected battlespace inside Venezuela—temporary control of a compound, approach/exit corridors, and air-defense environment—to seize Maduro and his wife and remove them to U.S. custody (PX-1, PX-2). Trump’s statements about overseeing Venezuela and controlling its oil infrastructure (PX-1, PX-3) and the scale of the operation (150+ aircraft, months of planning) show intent to establish control over that portion of Venezuelan territory. Rubio’s ‘arrest operation’ label is inconsistent with his admission that DoW forces hit threats going in, on the way out, and while on the ground in non-friendly territory (PX-3), which is offensive territorial control, not mere law enforcement. While there was no sustained occupation (PX-3, PX-4), the proposition requires ‘any portion,’ not permanent control. Temporary seizure of a fortified location by military force satisfies the definition under a preponderance standard.

The admitted record shows U.S. forces conducted a large‑scale offensive, seized a fortified compound in Caracas, and statements from officials indicated intent to control that area and influence Venezuela’s transition. This satisfies the proposition’s requirement of a military offensive intended to establish control over a portion of Venezuelan territory. On a preponderance standard, the evidence therefore demonstrates the proposition.

The admitted record (PX/DX-1 War Dept article, PX/DX-2 Brookings, PX/DX-3 LIG report, PX/DX-4 ABC transcript) proves a serious U.S. armed offensive in Caracas on Jan 2-3, 2026 within the window: 150+ aircraft, strikes on Venezuelan air-defense/military targets, a ground extraction force, gunfight, and removal of Maduro. That establishes a military offensive and temporary tactical dominance. But the proposition's decisive element is intent to establish control over a portion of Venezuelan sovereign territory, and that is not shown by a preponderance. Every primary/official source frames the objective as capture and extraction of persons, not holding land. Rubio described force protection for an arrest ('hit anything that was a threat' going in, out, and on the ground); AP reported no visible U.S. presence and no sign the U.S. was running Venezuela; Brookings noted no large-scale ground force, no direct governing role, and much of the regime remaining in place. The 'run Venezuela'/oil-quarantine statements show coercive influence over policy and succession, not intent to hold territory. Securing a compound and air corridor to complete an extraction is a means of the raid, not proof of territorial-control intent; treating it as such would erase the proposition's separate control element. Credible sources converge that force occurred but not on territorial-control intent. Plaintiff has not met the burden.

The preponderance of evidence shows U.S. forces established temporary territorial control during Operation Absolute Resolve, satisfying the proposition's requirement of military offensive intent to control any portion of Venezuela. Official sources confirm offensive actions against Venezuelan defenses, ground operations in Caracas, and post-operation assertions of control.

rationale: The record shows a significant U.S. military operation, a brief two‑hour ground presence, blockade and oil‑revenue custody, and statements about running Venezuela, but official sources (Rubio, Brookings) explicitly deny an invasion and frame the action as a capture/extraction and counter‑narcotics effort. No clear evidence demonstrates that the United States intended to establish control over any portion of Venezuelan land beyond the temporary site needed to seize Maduro. Under the preponderance standard, the intent element remains unproven, so the proposition is not demonstrated.

The admitted record (DX-1, PX-2, PX-3, PX-5) shows a planned, integrated military offensive—Operation Absolute Resolve—in which U.S. armed forces struck targets in and around Caracas and, backed by 150+ aircraft and multi-domain operations, seized and held a fortified site in the capital for a two-hour ground presence to capture and remove Venezuela's effective ruler, which under the proposition's broad 'any portion' and temporary-control standard is control over a portion of Venezuelan territory as the intended object of the offensive. Official capture/counter-narcotics framing and Rubio's 'not an invasion' label do not negate these admitted underlying facts, and the announced plan to 'run'/administer Venezuela's transition under threat of force further corroborates territorial-control intent. On a preponderance standard the proposition is more likely than not true.

The preponderance of admitted evidence shows the U.S. military offensive intended to establish control over Venezuelan territory. The Caracas operation controlled a portion of Venezuela, and the subsequent control of oil revenues and transition oversight demonstrate intent. Temporary control suffices.

rationale: The admitted record proves a major U.S. military operation on Venezuelan soil during the relevant window: strikes, a naval buildup, CIA operations, a December 2025 direct operation, and the January 3, 2026 Caracas raid capturing Maduro. However, the proposition requires more than force or presence—it requires a military offensive intended to establish control over any portion of Venezuelan territory. Official sources consistently frame the operation as capture, extraction, and joint military/law-enforcement (PX-2, DX-1), explicitly stating it was not an invasion or occupation. While transition language exists, it describes political oversight, not territorial administration. The record lacks any evidence of maps of controlled territory, orders establishing military government, checkpoints, occupied facilities, or forces remaining to administer land. Under a preponderance standard, the defense's official intent evidence is not overcome by plaintiff's secondary sources and ambiguous transition language. Therefore, territorial-control intent is not proved by a preponderance.

PX-1, PX-2, PX-3, and even defense exhibit DX-1 converge that on January 3, 2026 U.S. armed forces launched a planned military offensive inside Venezuela—200+ special operators and 150+ aircraft that forcibly suppressed Venezuelan air defenses to create and control a helicopter corridor into Venezuelan territory and dominated the fortified Fort Tiuna complex against Venezuela's will to seize and remove Maduro. The proposition requires only intended control over 'any portion,' not occupation or governance, and deliberately dominating that corridor and objective site by armed force satisfies that element regardless of the mixed law-enforcement framing. On a preponderance standard this is more likely true than not.

The admitted evidence demonstrates by preponderance that the January 3, 2026 Caracas operation constituted a military offensive intended to establish control over portions of Venezuelan territory. PX-1 confirms U.S. military control of a fortified site, DX-1 shows deliberate creation of controlled corridors, and the scale of operations indicates territorial control objectives as required by the proposition.

The admitted record shows U.S. military forces deliberately created temporary control over specific portions of Venezuelan territory (air corridors, specific sites) to execute Operation Absolute Resolve in Caracas. PX-1 confirms a 'highly fortified military fortress in the heart of Caracas' with integrated air-ground operations and 150+ aircraft. DX-1 states air defense batteries were attacked to create helicopter corridors entering Venezuelan territory. This intentional control of specific terrain to accomplish the mission satisfies 'any portion' per the proposition. The standard is preponderance of evidence, not permanent occupation.

The admitted record establishes by a preponderance that the United States commenced a military offensive on January 3, 2026 in Caracas, Venezuela. PX-1 (official U.S. Department of War) admits an 'extraordinary military operation in the capital of Venezuela' against a 'heavily fortified military fortress,' using joint forces, special operations, integrated air/ground/space/maritime operations, 150+ aircraft, and a ground extraction force. PX-2 (PBS/AP) independently corroborates a 'large-scale strike' across Caracas culminating in capture and removal. DX-1 (CSIS imagery) confirms >200 U.S. special operations forces surged through Caracas with battle damage at Fort Tiuna, La Carlota Air Base, La Guaira Port, and Higuerote Airport, and notes air defense batteries and radars were attacked to create a helicopter corridor into Venezuelan territory. These credible sources converge on U.S. military force deliberately controlling portions of Venezuelan territory—target sites and access corridors—to execute the raid. The proposition requires control over 'any portion,' not permanent occupation; the record shows intentional control as part of the military offensive.

The record shows U.S. forces entered Venezuela and captured Maduro, but no admitted source demonstrates an intent to establish control over any part of Venezuelan territory; sources describe a narrow, law‑enforcement raid, not a territorial‑control offensive.

The admitted evidence shows the US military operation was primarily a capture and extraction mission, falling under the proposition's express exclusion for head-of-state capture without territorial occupation. Official sources consistently describe the operation as limited in duration and law enforcement-focused. Statements regarding government removal appear to be political aftermath rather than the operational objective of the ground forces.

The admitted official record confirms U.S. ground forces entered Venezuelan territory in a combat-capable operation, but all sources consistently characterize it as a short-duration raid/extraction to apprehend Maduro for prosecution (PX-2, DX-1, DX-2). The proposition explicitly states 'Capture of a head of state without territorial occupation: NO.' The record shows no admission of territorial occupation, holding territory, or operational control of Caracas after the extraction. PX-1's 'oversee Venezuela' language describes political aftermath, not proof of ground forces establishing control during the operation. The defense's position is supported by the explicit edge case and consistent source language.

The admitted official record proves U.S. ground troops entered Caracas in a combat-capable operation before January 31, 2026. However, the proposition requires a qualifying objective: seizing or holding territory, removing the sitting government, or establishing operational control of a major population center. The proposition explicitly excludes capture of a head of state without territorial occupation and special operations raids without territorial objectives. The most precise official statements (PX-2, DX-1, DX-2) consistently characterize the objective as a law-enforcement operation to apprehend and transport Maduro and Flores for prosecution—a raid, capture, and arrest. While PX-1 mentions Maduro being 'out of power' and U.S. 'oversight,' these are political aftermath, not proof that ground troops had a qualifying territorial or operational-control objective. Plaintiff has not carried the preponderance burden on the objective element.

Official Department of War and White House sources (PX-1) confirm U.S. ground forces entered Caracas in combat, captured Maduro, and the President declared Maduro "now out of power" with U.S. oversight of a transition, satisfying the objective of removing the sitting government. The record meets the preponderance standard for the proposition's qualifying objectives, thus the event resolves YES.

Entry and combat capacity are met by a preponderance: official sources confirm U.S. ground personnel in Caracas (PX-1 ground-based extraction force; DX-1 'on the ground for about two hours'; DX-2 nearly 200 Americans downtown Caracas) in a combat-capable operation before the Jan 31, 2026 expiration. But the decisive element—a qualifying objective of seizing/holding territory, removing the sitting government, or establishing operational control of a major population center—is not proven. The most precise official statements describe a short, targeted capture/extraction and law-enforcement mission to apprehend and transport Maduro for prosecution (PX-2 'targeted and limited... law enforcement operation to apprehend and transport'; DX-1 'not an invasion,' 'not an extended military operation,' 'raid and... capture and... arrest'; DX-2 'grabbed an indicted individual... in support of law enforcement'). PX-1's 'out of power' and 'oversee Venezuela' language is political/diplomatic aftermath—no admitted source shows U.S. ground troops holding territory or exercising operational control of Caracas, and a Venezuelan VP was sworn in to govern. The proposition's express edge cases resolve NO for capture of a head of state without territorial occupation and for special-operations raids without territorial objectives; reading a bare capture as automatic government removal would nullify that carveout. On the whole official record, the required territorial/operational-control objective is unproven, so the proposition does not resolve YES by a preponderance.
