rationale: Official sources PX-1 and PX-2 confirm Jan 3 2026 US ground raid in Caracas captured Maduro, explicitly characterized as capture/arrest to bring to justice, not invasion. Proposition edge case excludes capture of head of state without territorial occupation. Record proves excluded category.

The admitted evidence (PX‑1, PX‑2) describes a raid and capture of President Maduro by a ground‑based extraction force in Caracas, but it provides no proof of seizing or holding Venezuelan territory, nor of establishing operational control of a major population center. The record therefore falls under the edge‑case exclusion ‘capture of a head of state without territorial occupation: NO.’ Consequently the proposition is not demonstrated by a preponderance of the evidence.

The admitted official sources (War.gov PX-1/DX-1; White House Rubio release PX-2/DX-2) prove U.S. ground forces (a ground-based extraction force plus special operations forces) entered Caracas in combat capacity before the Jan. 31, 2026 expiration. But the proposition requires that the operation's objective be seizing/holding territory, removing the sitting government, or establishing operational control of a major population center, and it expressly excludes 'special operations raids without territorial objectives' and 'Capture of a head of state without territorial occupation: NO.' Every official statement of the operation's purpose describes a capture/extraction/arrest mission: 'to bring outlaw dictator Nicolas Maduro to justice,' the 'mission to capture the Maduros,' a 'massive joint military and law enforcement raid,' with Maduro flown out aboard USS Iwo Jima. The White House affirmatively states it was 'not an invasion' and 'not an extended military operation' and calls it 'this raid and this capture and this arrest.' No admitted source states U.S. ground forces seized, held, occupied, administered, or controlled Caracas or any Venezuelan territory. Plaintiff's removal-of-government/transition-control theory rests on post-capture political aftermath (oversight of a transition, continued pressure, a successor willing to cooperate), not on the operation's objective or any territorial occupation; adopting it would nullify the express head-of-state-capture edge case. By a preponderance, the record shows an excluded capture raid, not the qualifying territorial or governmental-control operation. There is no DoD/White House conflict triggering the UN fallback.

The record shows Operation Absolute Resolve was exclusively a capture/extraction mission targeting Maduro, as confirmed by official sources consistently describing it as a 'raid' and explicitly denying it was an invasion. The subsequent political consequences don't establish a territorial control objective, placing this under the proposition's exclusion for head-of-state capture without territorial occupation.

The admitted official record proves U.S. ground forces entered Caracas in combat capacity but fails to prove a qualifying objective under the proposition's explicit criteria. The consistent official characterization (PX-1, PX-2, PX-4, DX-1) frames the operation as a capture/extraction raid to bring Maduro to justice. PX-1's post-capture language about Maduro being 'out of power' and U.S. 'overseeing' Venezuela describes political consequences, not the ground force's stated objective. The White House explicitly denies invasion or extended operation (PX-2). There is no admitted evidence of territorial occupation. The proposition's edge case—capture of a head of state without territorial occupation resolves NO—directly controls this scenario. The plaintiff has not carried the burden of proving a qualifying objective by a preponderance.

The admitted record confirms U.S. forces entered Caracas for a short combat raid that captured Maduro. However, official White House statements limit the operation to a two‑hour capture, deny an invasion or extended occupation, and characterize it as law‑enforcement. While the Defense Department release mentions Maduro being ‘out of power’ and U.S. oversight, it does not state the ground operation’s objective was to remove the sitting government, nor does it show territorial or operational‑control objectives. Under the proposition’s edge‑case carve‑out, a capture of a head of state without territorial occupation resolves NO. Accordingly, the preponderance of the evidence does not satisfy any qualifying objective, so the proposition does not resolve YES.

The admitted official record proves only a roughly two-hour U.S. ground raid in Caracas to capture Maduro (PX-1, PX-2), expressly framed by official sources as a capture/extraction/arrest/law-enforcement action and 'not an invasion' or 'extended military operation,' with no occupation, retained territory, or operational control of the city. The proposition's controlling edge case makes capture of a head of state without territorial occupation resolve NO, and reading 'removing the sitting government' to swallow any capture would nullify that carve-out and the raid-without-territorial-objective exclusion. Post-capture 'oversee Venezuela' transition language is political aftermath, not proof the ground force entered with a qualifying territorial, governmental-removal, or operational-control objective, so plaintiff fails the qualifying-objective element by a preponderance.

The admitted evidence proves a combat-capable ground entry but fails to demonstrate a qualifying objective beyond the proposition's explicit carve-out for head-of-state capture without territorial occupation. Official sources consistently characterize the operation as a short-duration capture/extraction mission, not governmental removal or territorial control.

The admitted record confirms a brief U.S. ground operation in Caracas for capturing Maduro (2 hours), with White House characterization as law enforcement, capture, and extradition. The proposition's edge case specifies capture of a head of state without territorial occupation resolves NO. There is no evidence that the operation had the objective of seizing territory, establishing operational control of a population center, or systematic removal of the government beyond the capture itself. The U.S. oversight of Venezuela post-operation reflects political aftermath, not the operation's objective. Therefore, the proposition does not resolve YES by preponderance of evidence.

The record contains multiple independent, credible sources (Guardian, Ukrainska Pravda, RFE/RL) that document a public, mutually‑confirmed announcement of a May 9‑11 cease‑fire, explicitly stating suspension of all kinetic activity. Under the market rule, such consensus reporting satisfies the requirement of an official, publicly announced, mutually agreed halt. While implementation evidence shows violations, that affects compliance, not the formation of the agreement. On the balance of evidence, it is more likely than not that the parties reached the qualifying cease‑fire, meeting the preponderance standard.

Under the market rule (PX-1/DX-1), a wide consensus of credible media reporting that an official ceasefire was reached suffices, and an agreement reached before the deadline qualifies regardless of later start or violations. Guardian, Ukrainska Pravda (Zelenskyy on X), and RFE/RL independently converge that Trump announced and both sides publicly confirmed a dated May 9-11 ceasefire 'in the war between Russia and Ukraine' with 'suspension of all kinetic activity'—general-conflict, not humanitarian, sector-specific, or future-framework language. The defense's continued-operations and mutual-violation evidence (DX-4/DX-5) bears on compliance, not formation, which the rule expressly excludes, so the record proves the proposition by a preponderance.

While both sides announced a ceasefire, the preponderance of evidence shows it was primarily a prisoner exchange arrangement tied to Victory Day rather than a general pause in the conflict. The Russian framing ('for prisoner exchange'), narrow Ukrainian decree (Red Square security), and continued fighting across frontlines indicate this did not meet the market's requirement for a general halt in military engagement.

Media consensus confirms an official, general ceasefire in the Russia-Ukraine conflict with suspension of all kinetic activity, meeting market criteria. Official source framing (prisoner exchange/Victory Day) does not negate the general scope announced publicly.

The admitted evidence proves a publicly announced May 9-11 arrangement but fails to establish it was a general pause in the conflict. Russia’s official statement framed the ceasefire as for conducting a prisoner exchange, the only clearly defined operational condition was Ukraine refraining from striking Red Square, and credible monitoring (ISW via NV and CBS/AP) reported continued offensive operations across the frontline from day one. While media used broad language like 'ceasefire in the war' and 'suspension of all kinetic activity,' the actual official context, undefined conditions, and battlefield implementation show a limited prisoner-exchange/Victory Day deconfliction. Under the market’s exclusions for limited sector-specific ceasefires and humanitarian pauses, and on a preponderance standard, plaintiff has not carried the burden.

A wide consensus of credible reporting (ABC, Guardian, China Daily/Xinhua, Ukrainska Pravda) plus the official-source record shows Russia and Ukraine mutually and publicly confirmed a dated May 9-11 ceasefire whose stated operative term was 'suspension of all kinetic activity'—theater-wide language, not a sector, Red Square, or humanitarian-only carveout—which the market rule expressly accepts as proof an official ceasefire was reached. The defense's decree (D-1) is one narrow implementation act, and ISW's report of continued but decreased operations goes to imperfect compliance, which the rule discounts by counting an agreement reached before the deadline regardless of later performance. On the preponderance standard the qualifying general-pause characterization is more likely than not.

The official Ukrainian decree (D-1) shows only a narrow Red Square targeting exclusion. Implementation evidence (D-2) documents continued theater-wide military operations. This arrangement constitutes a humanitarian pause tied to prisoner exchange, not a general conflict pause as required by the proposition.

Record shows official mutual agreement for May 9-11 ceasefire with 'suspension of all kinetic activity' as confirmed by multiple credible sources. Agreement structured as dated, theater-wide halt rather than narrow humanitarian pause. Imperfect compliance during implementation does not negate the existence of a qualifying ceasefire agreement. Market rule permits media consensus to establish official mutuality.

The direct Ukrainian official decree is explicitly narrow—limiting actions only to Red Square exclusion during the May 9 parade, not a theater-wide halt. The Russian official source frames the ceasefire as purpose-limited for prisoner exchange. The ISW assessment confirms continued limited offensive operations across the theater during May 9-11, with activity decreasing but not ceasing. While multiple credible reports used broad suspension of all kinetic activity language, the most direct official sources and implementation evidence undercut the qualifying general pause in the conflict characterization. The market rule excludes limited arrangements. Under preponderance, the non-qualifying characterization is at least as likely, so plaintiff has not met their burden.

Multiple credible sources (ABC News, The Guardian, China Daily) directly quote both Presidents and Kremlin officials stating a three‑day cease‑fire from May 9‑11 with a suspension of all kinetic activity. The market rule permits a wide consensus of credible media reporting as proof of an official agreement. Although the Ukrainian presidential decree is narrowly scoped, it does not contradict the broader cease‑fire announcement, and implementation reports of violations do not negate the existence of the agreement. On the balance of the record, it is more likely than not that Russia and Ukraine publicly and mutually agreed to a general pause in military engagement before May 31, satisfying the proposition at the preponderance standard.

The evidence shows the May arrangement was limited to prisoner exchange and parade security, not a general halt in military engagement. Battlefield monitoring confirms continued operations across the theater, and the Ukrainian decree was geographically restricted. The plaintiff has not met the burden of proof by a preponderance of the evidence.

ISW battlefield monitoring shows continued offensive operations across the theater during the May 9-11 ceasefire period, including artillery, drone strikes, and ongoing deployments. Official Russian and Ukrainian sources frame the arrangement as prisoner-exchange and parade-specific, not a general pause in military engagement. The record lacks evidence of a theater-wide halt, meeting the rule's exclusions for limited agreements.

The admitted record shows that Russia and Ukraine publicly announced a temporary May 9-11 arrangement, but the direct official sources frame it as limited: Russia's official statement (ev_959b165782b3) explicitly ties the ceasefire to conducting a prisoner exchange, and Ukraine's presidential decree (ev_cf6a5f80e566) is narrowly scoped to permitting the Victory Day parade and excluding Red Square targeting. Battlefield monitoring (ev_56be60eae740, ev_672e50c14e62) reports continued limited offensive operations, rotations, and logistics across the theater on May 9-10, with activity decreased but not ceased. Secondary reporting of a 'suspension of all kinetic activity' comes from media quoting statements, not direct primary sources. The market rule requires a general pause in the conflict and excludes humanitarian pauses and limited agreements. The combination of purpose-limited official framing, geographically narrow Ukrainian decree, and continued theater-wide hostilities supports that this arrangement did not_constitute a qualifying general halt by a preponderance.

The record contains an official Russian acceptance of a ceasefire and multiple credible media reports of a mutually announced three‑day pause, but the Ukrainian presidential decree is narrow and battlefield monitoring (ISW reports) shows continued military operations across the theater during May 9‑11. Under the market rule, a general pause requires suspension of kinetic activity broadly; the evidence of ongoing combat indicates the ceasefire was limited, not a qualifying general halt. Therefore the proposition is not demonstrated by preponderance.

The market rule requires a mutually agreed general halt in military engagement and excludes humanitarian or limited arrangements, but the only direct official sources are limited in scope: the Russian statement frames the ceasefire as 'for the purpose of conducting a prisoner exchange,' and the direct Ukrainian decree is a humanitarian-purposed Red Square parade targeting carveout, not a theater-wide halt. The affirmative 'suspension of all kinetic activity' language rests on secondary reporting of Trump's characterization, while the independent ISW monitoring for both May 9 and May 10 reports continued limited offensive operations across the theater, mutual violation accusations, and activity that decreased but did not cease. On a preponderance standard this record shows a temporary, exchange- and Victory-Day-centered arrangement rather than a qualifying general pause in the conflict.

The admitted record proves all three elements by a preponderance: (1) Public availability by May 8 is conclusively established—multiple credible outlets (AP/PBS, Courthouse News, FOX Local, Reuters, NPR, BBC, Guardian) reported on May 6–7, 2026 that Judge Kenneth Karas unsealed/released a document described as a purported Epstein suicide note. (2) Credible reporting of Epstein authorship exists—the SDNY order identifies it as an alleged suicide note purportedly authored by Epstein, and AP handwriting analysis reports experts found common authorship with another Epstein-associated note (one stating same author). Hedging language (purported/alleged) is standard for responsible reporting of contested documents. (3) Intent is supported—the text includes 'choose one’s time to say goodbye,' recovered after a suspected suicide attempt with physical evidence of self-harm, consistently characterized by outlets as a purported suicide note. The market rule explicitly adopts a consensus-of-credible-reporting standard, not forensic authentication. On this record, the proposition is more likely true than false.

The record shows multiple reputable outlets (AP/PBS, Reuters, NPR, BBC, Guardian, Courthouse News, FOX Local) publicly reporting before May 8, 2026 that a court‑unsealed handwritten note was released and described as a purported Jeffrey Epstein suicide note or farewell‑equivalent. The official SDNY order confirms public release. AP’s handwriting analysis adds credible reporting of common authorship with another Epstein‑associated note. While caveats (purported, alleged) and lack of formal authentication exist, the consensus of credible reporting meets the market rule’s “credibly reported” standard. By preponderance, it is more likely than not that the note was credibly reported as written by Epstein and intended as a suicide note or equivalent communication.

The market rule (PX-1/PX-R4/D-5) sets the test as credible reporting, not official authentication, and allows public availability by any means; resolution is by a consensus of credible reporting. Public availability before May 8 is proven and conceded: the SDNY unsealing order dated May 6 (PX-R1/DS-1) and contemporaneous credible reports (AP/PBS May 6-7 [PX-4], Courthouse News May 6 [PX-5], FOX Local May 6 [PX-6], plus Reuters/NPR/BBC/Guardian [PX-2]) show the note was unsealed and made public. Authorship is credibly reported: multiple reputable outlets described a note purported/alleged to be written by Epstein, tied to the court-release chain, and the defense's own AP handwriting exhibit (PX-R3/DS-3) affirmatively supports common authorship with another Epstein-cell note (one examiner: 'the same person') and notes phrasing echoing Epstein's prior emails. Intent as a suicide note or equivalent is credibly reported: the note was recovered after a suspected jail hanging attempt, contains 'time to say goodbye,' 'NO FUN,' 'NOT WORTH IT,' and was repeatedly characterized as a purported suicide note; the rule's 'final message, or equivalent communication' language is met even if the exact 'suicide note' label is contested. The defense's caveats (no DOJ authentication, DOJ first-viewing, Tartaglione custody chain, grievance-note comparator) reduce certainty but do not outweigh converging credible reporting under the rule's credible-reporting standard. On preponderance, the proposition is more likely true than false.

While public availability is established, the preponderance of evidence does not show credible reporting of Epstein's authorship or suicide/final-message intent due to unresolved authentication issues, source chain weaknesses, and ambiguous context.

The record shows that by May 6, 2026, a document was publicly released by Judge Karas' SDNY order (PX-4) and widely reported by mainstream outlets (PX-5 Reuters Connect, PX-6 AP/PBS, PX-7 Guardian). These sources describe it as a 'suicide note purportedly written by Epstein' or 'alleged suicide note written by Epstein' - satisfying the 'credibly reported to have been written by Jeffrey Epstein' element. The same reports characterize it as a suicide/alleged suicide note, covering the 'intended as a suicide note, final message, or equivalent communication' element. While the defense evidence (DB-1) shows DOJ had not authenticated the note and it originated through Tartaglione's proceedings, the market rule (PX-1) specifies resolution by 'consensus of credible reporting.' Multiple credible outlets before May 8 reported the note's existence, authorship attribution, and qualifying intent. Under a preponderance standard, this is more likely than not true.

Multiple mainstream outlets (Reuters, NPR/AP, BBC, Guardian) reported the court‑unsealed document before May 8, 2026, describing it as a suicide note *purported* to be written by Jeffrey Epstein and quoting language about choosing one’s time to say goodbye. The market rule requires only credible reporting of such a note, not authenticated authorship. The consistent, contemporaneous reporting meets the preponderance standard.

The rule (PX-1) resolves on a consensus of credible reporting, not conclusive authentication, and by May 6-7 the note was publicly available (PX-4 SDNY unsealing order, PX-5 Reuters/SDNY handout, PX-6 AP/PBS, PX-7 Guardian) before the May 8 deadline. Those same credible sources plus the official court order reported the document as a purported/alleged Epstein suicide note quoting 'a treat to be able to choose one's time to say goodbye,' satisfying credible reporting of Epstein authorship and suicide/final-message intent by a preponderance. The DOJ non-authentication and chain-of-custody caveats (DB-1) bear on actual-authorship certainty, which the rule does not require, so the proposition is more likely true than false.

The admitted record shows credible reporting consensus that a note purportedly written by Epstein and framed as a suicide note/final message was publicly available by May 8. Under the market rule's reporting standard, this satisfies the proposition by preponderance of evidence.

The record shows a public court release of a document before May 8, with multiple credible outlets reporting it as alleged/purported suicide note. Consistent caveats in reporting: 'not authenticated,' 'no signature,' 'DOJ never had it,' 'unclear authorship,' 'not verified,' and 'first time DOJ saw it.' Credible reporting preserved these doubts, but the proposition requires credibly reported authorship and intent—not hedged reporting preserving uncertainty. Preponderance not met.

The record includes a court order unsealing a document described as a suicide note purportedly authored by Jeffrey Epstein, and multiple reputable outlets (Reuters, NPR, BBC, AP) reported the release before May 8, 2026, characterizing it as Epstein’s suicide note or goodbye note. While authentication and chain‑of‑custody are noted as incomplete, the market rule requires only credible reporting of authorship and intent, which the consensus of these sources provides. By a preponderance, the proposition is satisfied.

The rule requires only credible reporting, by preponderance, that a note written by Epstein and intended as a suicide/final message was publicly available by May 8; PX-1's May 6 SDNY order unsealed the note and Reuters (5/6), NPR, BBC, and AP (5/7) all publicly reported it as Epstein's purported suicide/goodbye note, with the text 'a treat to be able to choose one's time to say goodbye' in a suspected-suicide context. The non-authentication, chain-of-custody, and DOJ 'not yet authenticated' caveats reduce certainty but do not negate credible reporting of authorship and intent, since the proposition uses the anticipated 'purported/credibly reported' standard rather than requiring authentication. The defense's position effectively demands proven authorship and intent, a higher bar than the proposition and preponderance burden require.

Public availability is established, but the record lacks credible reporting of Epstein's authorship or suicide-note intent due to unverified sourcing, authentication caveats, and ambiguity in the note's content.

The admitted record shows public release of a document described as a purported suicide note, but lacks authentication of authorship or chain of custody. DOJ stated it had not seen the note before the court release, and the provenance comes through Tartaglione who had a self-protective motive. While the note contains language about 'choosing one's time to say goodbye,' the lack of authentication means the preponderance standard for both authorship and intent is not met.

The record establishes by a preponderance that by May 8, 2026, a note was publicly available and credibly reported as written by Jeffrey Epstein and intended as a suicide note or equivalent. PX-1 shows Judge Karas's May 6 SDNY order unsealing the note described as a 'suicide note purportedly authored by Jeffrey Epstein.' PX-2 (Reuters May 6), PX-3 (NPR May 7), PX-4 (BBC May 7), and DX-1 (AP) corroborate the release and characterize it as Epstein's purported suicide note, with preserved text including 'It is a treat to be able to choose one's time to say goodbye.' The hedging ('purportedly') and lack of authentication affect weight, not the proposition, which asks for credible reporting, not conclusive proof. The consensus of major outlets, plus official court release, meets the preponderance standard.

The rule requires only that a note be credibly reported—not conclusively authenticated—as written by Epstein and intended as a suicide note or final-message equivalent, and made public by May 8; PX-2's SDNY order unsealed the note before the deadline and PX-3 (Guardian) and PX-4 (AP) credibly reported it before the deadline as an alleged/attributed Epstein suicide note, with quoted text ('choose one's time to say goodbye,' 'NOT WORTH IT') found after his July 2019 suicide attempt satisfying the intent element. The adverse caveats (unsigned, DOJ non-authentication, Mark Epstein's forgery opinion, Tartaglione custody) reduce weight but do not outweigh a court-unsealed note reported by two major outlets, so the proposition is more likely true than false under preponderance.

While a purported note was publicly available before the deadline, the record contains substantial unresolved doubts about authorship (unsigned, unverified by sources, DOJ non-authentication, forgery claim) and intent (ambiguous text, connection to prior incident). The preponderance of evidence does not establish credible reporting that Jeffrey Epstein wrote the note or intended it as a suicide note/final message under the market rule.

The admitted record contains no credible verification that Jeffrey Epstein authored the note or that it was intended as a suicide note or final message. Multiple sources explicitly state it was unverified, unauthenticated, not in DOJ files, and Mark Epstein called it a forgery. While the document was public by the deadline, the "credibly reported" standard requires more than publication of an alleged note - it requires credible evidence for authorship and intent.

The admitted evidence establishes by a preponderance that, by May 8, 2026, 11:59 PM ET, a note was made publicly available and credibly reported to have been written by Jeffrey Epstein and intended as a suicide note or equivalent communication. PX-2 (SDNY order dated May 6, 2026) proves official unsealing. PX-3 (The Guardian, first published May 6) and PX-4 (AP, published May 7) provide independent, credible pre-deadline reporting that the unsealed note was written by/attributed to Epstein and characterized as a suicide note, with quoted text demonstrating intent ("choose one’s time to say goodbye", "NO FUN - NOT WORTH IT!!"). The rule (PX-1) accepts "credibly reported" rather than conclusively authenticated. While authorship remains uncertain (unsigned, not DOJ-verified, disputed provenance via Tartaglione), the credible-reporting standard is satisfied by multiple reputable sources disclosing caveats yet still attributing the note to Epstein as a suicide/final-message equivalent. Preponderance weighs in favor of the proposition.

The record shows a federal court unsealed a document described as an alleged Jeffrey Epstein suicide note before the May 8 deadline, and reputable news outlets (The Guardian and AP) credibly reported it as such, including its intent language. While authenticity was not definitively established, the rule only requires credible reporting, which the sources provided. Thus, by a preponderance of the evidence, the proposition is demonstrated.

The plaintiff has established by a preponderance of evidence that a note meeting the market criteria was credibly reported and publicly available before the deadline. PX-2 proves judicial unsealing on May 6, 2026 of a note specifically described as a purported Epstein suicide note. Contemporaneous reports (PX-3, PX-4) confirm public availability and reproduce qualifying text. While genuine authenticity doubts exist (acknowledged in PX-4 and DX-1), the market rule only requires credible reporting of the required elements, not conclusive authenticity. The evidence clears this threshold.

The admitted record shows public availability of a disputed item but does not establish by a preponderance that the item was credibly reported to have been written by Epstein and intended as a suicide note. All reporting includes explicit caveats about authorship, verification status, and provenance. The ruling requires credible reporting of authorship and intent, not just public availability of a contested document.

The record establishes that a court-unsealed document was made public before May 8, 2026, and was reported by credible outlets (FOX Local, People, AP/PBS). However, the admitted reporting consistently frames the document as a purported/alleged suicide note and explicitly states that authorship is unclear, the note was undated/unsigned/unverified. Under the market rule, a qualifying note must be credibly reported to have been written by Jeffrey Epstein. The admitted reporting does not credibly establish Epstein's authorship; it credibly reports that authorship remains disputed. Because authorship is an essential element not proved by a preponderance, the proposition is not demonstrated.

The record shows a federal court order unsealing a document described as an alleged Jeffrey Epstein suicide note, and multiple contemporaneous reputable news outlets (FOX Local, People, PBS/AP) reported the note before the May 8 deadline, credibly attributing authorship to Epstein and intent as a suicide/farewell communication. While authorship and verification remain disputed, the market rule requires only credible reporting, not certainty. On a preponderance basis, the evidence satisfies the proposition that a qualifying note was publicly available and credibly reported by the deadline.

The SDNY order (PX-2) unsealed a note described as a suicide note purportedly authored by Epstein, and multiple credible outlets (FOX, People, AP/PBS) publicly reported the release on May 6, 2026, before the deadline, identifying it as a purported Epstein note with farewell text ('choose one's time to say goodbye'). The rule requires credible reporting of authorship and suicide/final-message intent, not official authentication, and the record supplies that by a preponderance. The unsigned/undated/unverified caveats reduce weight but do not defeat the credible reporting the rule contemplates.

The record shows public availability before the deadline (SDNY order May 6 and AP/PBS reporting). The evidence contains credible reporting that the note was attributed to Epstein (court order describes as 'purportedly authored by Epstein', AP reports cellmate claimed it was found) and has content consistent with a suicide/final message. The rule requires 'credibly reported to have been written', not forensic certainty. The caveats in evidence (e.g. 'unclear who wrote') are part of credible reporting, not disproof.

The admitted record establishes by a preponderance that before May 8, 2026, 11:59 PM ET, a note credibly reported to have been written by Epstein and intended as a suicide/final message was made publicly available. PX-2 (SDNY order, May 6, 2026) directed unsealing of an alleged suicide note purportedly authored by Epstein, proving court-ordered public release before the deadline via the clerk's docketing. PX-3 (AP/PBS, published May 6, 8:01 PM EDT) reports the note was made public Wednesday, describes its content (choose the time to say goodbye; NO FUN; NOT WORTH IT), and ties it to Epstein's first suspected jail suicide attempt (July 23, 2019), satisfying the suicide/final-message intent element. The rule requires credible reporting of authorship and intent, not forensic certainty or official authentication. Despite caveats in PX-3 (it is unclear who wrote the note) and D-1 (Mark Epstein's forgery challenge), the court's unsealing order and major wire-service reporting—paired with the note's context and reported language—are sufficiently credible under a preponderance standard.

The record shows public availability before the deadline, but all sources describing authorship qualify it as alleged, purported, or unclear, and a family member alleges forgery. No source credibly reports that Jeffrey Epstein wrote the note; the evidence does not meet the preponderance standard for authorship and intent required by the proposition.

Under the rule's own standard ('credibly reported to have been written by Epstein and intended as a suicide note, final message, or equivalent'), the admitted record proves the proposition by a preponderance. Public availability before the May 8 11:59pm ET deadline is firmly shown: the SDNY order (May 6, PX-2) directed docketing/unsealing of the Note, and AP reporting carried by PBS (PX-3, published May 6, updated May 7) reported it was made public, with a Reuters-distributed handout image described as 'a note purported to be written by Jeffrey Epstein.' Credible authorship reporting is met: a federal court order and a major wire service (AP/PBS) both credibly reported and released the document as purportedly authored by Epstein, recovered after his July 23, 2019 suspected suicide attempt. The rule requires credible reporting, not forensic authentication. Qualifying intent is met: the reported content ('choose the time to say goodbye,' 'NO FUN,' 'NOT WORTH IT!!'), in the context of an event the IG characterized as a first suicide attempt, supports a suicide/final/equivalent message. The caveats ('alleged'/'purported,' AP's 'unclear who wrote the note,' absence from DOJ/IG files, and Mark Epstein's forgery claim in D-1) reduce weight, but D-1's own counterweights (sealed in May 2021 before the Little Rascals emails surfaced in 2026; multiple people attempted authentication in 2019-2020; Tartaglione described it before the files' release) blunt the forgery theory. On balance, credible public reporting of an Epstein-attributed final/suicide message, available before the deadline, is more likely than not.

Public availability was established by PX-2/PX-3, but the record shows insufficient credible reporting of Epstein authorship given source chain issues, prominent caveats in evidence, and absence from official investigations.

The admitted record contains explicit, context-rich denials that any qualifying announcement occurred. Times of India's fact-check states there was no proof, no confirmed girlfriend, no medical update, no video confirming a pregnancy, and no statement from Clavicular confirming it, and it deems the viral pregnancy claim false. Hindustan Times and The Express Tribune likewise report only future-oriented remarks ('I must have children... I will be a dad in the next few months') tied to a pregnancy-contest stunt. Forbes' partial fragment about 'gotten a girl pregnant' is the sole plaintiff Support, but it is incomplete and contradicted by the fuller, preserved reporting. Under a preponderance standard, the clearer, more complete admitted evidence weighs against the proposition. The rule also excludes non-credible announcements such as jokes or stunts, and the preserved record characterizes the remarks this way. There is no preserved Clavicular statement meeting the rule's credible-announcement requirement; therefore the proposition is not demonstrated.

The admitted record lacks any primary statement from Clavicular or a representative confirming a pregnancy. Forbes provides only a fragment and conflicting media (Times of India, Hindustan Times, Express Tribune) describe non‑qualifying remarks or unverified rumors. The rule requires a credible announcement; jokes, contests, or future‑fatherhood statements do not satisfy it. Without a definitive consensus of credible media, the preponderance standard is not met.

On the admitted record the proposition is not shown by a preponderance. The rule requires a credible announcement by Clavicular of an existing partner pregnancy, excludes jokes/non-credible claims, and permits media reporting only as a definitive consensus. The primary/platform evidence (PX-1/PX-7) proves only the rule and a disputed final Yes state; the Forbes exhibit itself notes 'the market description and platform clarification say No; the price says Yes,' so the oracle outcome is not clean proof of the fact. The lone favorable item is an incomplete Forbes snippet ('gotten a girl pregnant') extracted from a metered page with no surrounding sentence, source chain, clip, or transcript, leaving it ambiguous whether it reports a confirmation, a disputed rumor, or the excluded joke. By contrast, the sources preserving actual context (PX-8 Times of India, Hindustan Times, Express Tribune) uniformly describe an unconfirmed viral girlfriend-pregnancy rumor ('no proof, no confirmed girlfriend, no medical update, no video confirming pregnancy, no statement from Clavicular confirming it') and future-oriented/contest remarks ('I will be a dad in the next few months'), with TOI concluding the claim was false and likely jokes/out-of-context clips. That is conflicting media, not a definitive consensus, and the only statements preserved with context are non-qualifying. Plaintiff's burden is therefore unmet.

The record lacks sufficient evidence of a credible pregnancy announcement. While the final market resolution and Forbes fragment suggest a qualifying statement, the preserved evidence shows only non-qualifying contest remarks and unconfirmed rumors. Temporary control still constitutes 'control' under the market's definition of 'military offensive intended to establish control.'

The admitted record proves by a preponderance that Israel commenced a military offensive intended to establish control over Syrian territory. Official Israeli statements (PX-2, PX-3, PX-5/D-3/D-4/D-6) explicitly describe orders to 'take over positions,' 'temporarily take control of the buffer zone in Syria,' and continued deployment on Mount Hermon until a security arrangement is found. UNDOF's 13 Dec press statement (PX-4/D-5) officially confirms significant IDF movements within the area of separation and that IDF remained in multiple locations, which it characterized as violating the 1974 Disengagement Agreement. Credible reporting (PX-6, PX-7) corroborates that the IDF took over and held the Syrian side of Mount Hermon. The market rules count the Golan Heights as Israeli territory but explicitly exclude the Syrian peak/side of Mount Hermon (PX-1). Israel’s own characterizations as 'temporary' and 'defensive' describe motive and duration, not the fact of control. The proposition requires intent to establish control, not permanence or annexation. The preponderance of official confirmation (Israel, UN) and credible reporting established the entry, holding, and intent to control Syrian territory.

By a preponderance, the admitted record proves all elements. Timing: AP (PX-1/ev-03) and Reuters/Arab News (DX-1/ev-05) show Israeli forces moved into the Syrian buffer zone and the Syrian side of Mount Hermon in December 2024, within the Sept 12-Dec 31 window, following Assad's Dec 8 fall; Netanyahu's Dec 17 visit is corroborated by AP (ev-01). Geography outside the Golan carve-out: AP places Netanyahu at the summit of Mount Hermon 'inside Syria, about 10 km from the Israel-held Golan Heights,' and Reuters confirms troops took over an abandoned Syrian military post on the Syrian side of Mount Hermon; the UN record (S/PV.9857, ev-02) has China urging withdrawal 'from Syrian territory' and France demanding respect for Syria's sovereignty. Offensive conduct and control: forces captured a ~400 sq km demilitarized area, established multiple positions, fortifications, bases, posts and a helipad (UNDOF/Syrian statements), and Israel's own FM conceded 'targeted and temporary control.' Intent to establish control: Netanyahu said forces would stay 'until another arrangement is found that ensures Israel's security,' Katz ordered a presence and fortifications for a possible extended stay, and the UN noted Israel's 'declared intent to maintain an indefinite presence.' The defense's temporary/defensive/no-permanent-presence framing bears on motive and duration, not on whether control was established; the market requires only a military offensive intended to establish control over any portion of Syria, not annexation or permanence. The proposition is demonstrated.

The preponderance of evidence shows Israeli forces entered Syrian territory outside the Golan Heights in December 2024, seized military positions including a Syrian post on Mount Hermon, fortified them, and declared intent to maintain control until security conditions were met. This satisfies the market's definition of a military offensive intended to establish control, regardless of temporary or defensive characterization.

The admitted record shows Israeli forces entered Syrian territory (Syrian side of Mount Hermon, not part of Golan Heights), took control of strategic positions, fortified them, and stated they would remain until security conditions were met. UN, Syrian, and Israeli official sources confirm violation of the 1974 agreement and establishment of control over Syrian territory. The market's definition requires control over any portion of Syria, not permanent annexation. Preponderance of evidence supports Yes.

The admitted record establishes by a preponderance that Israel commenced a military offensive intended to establish control over Syrian territory during the September 12-December 31 2024 window. PX-1 and PX-2 show Israeli troops entered the Syrian side of Mount Hermon and the UN buffer zone in December 2024 captured positions and announced intent to remain until a security arrangement. PX-3 and PX-4 provide official UN confirmation of IDF positions in the Syrian area of separation. Defense DX-1 concedes presence and takeover of a Syrian military post. Temporary or defensive characterization does not negate established control. Proposition demonstrated by preponderance.

The admitted record includes multiple contemporaneous AP reports, Israeli statements, and UN Security Council confirmations showing Israeli forces entered Syrian territory (the Syrian side of Mount Hermon) during December 2024, took and held positions, and announced intent to remain until security arrangements were in place. This satisfies the market’s definition of a military offensive intended to establish control over a portion of Syria. Under a preponderance standard, the evidence more likely than not proves the proposition.

The evidence shows a clear military operation, but fails to prove the required territorial control intent by a preponderance. Official sources describe the mission as capture/extraction (PX-1, PX-2), and operational details confirm tactical force protection rather than territorial control (PX-3). Post-operation evidence shows no sustained presence or administrative governance (ABC observation, Brookings). The 'protected battlespace' argument conflates tactical dominance with territorial control intent.

error: Council member C1 agent process exited before completing opportunity deliberation:1:C1.

rationale: The record proves a serious military operation in Caracas: U.S. forces used over 150 aircraft, targeted air-defense and military sites, and extracted Maduro from a fortified compound. However, official sources repeatedly frame the objective as capture and extraction (PX-1: joint military and law-enforcement mission to capture the Maduros; PX-2: captured from a fortified compound and transported to USS Iwo Jima). Secretary Rubio’s contemporaneous explanation, preserved in PX-3, states explicitly it was an arrest/law-enforcement operation with War Department forces protecting agents going in, on the ground, and exiting—not an intent to hold or administer Venezuelan territory. ABC’s transcript adds credible contemporaneous detail but also preserves that AP saw no visible sign of a U.S. presence or the U.S. running Venezuela afterward. PX-4 corroborates that there was no large ground force to run the country and much of the Venezuelan regime remained in place. Together, credible sources converge on a targeted extraction with temporary tactical dominance, not an intent to establish control over Venezuelan territory. On a preponderance standard, the plaintiff has not shown that the military offensive was intended to establish control over any portion of Venezuela.

The admitted record shows that U.S. forces conducted a coordinated military operation in Caracas, using more than 150 aircraft and special‑operations forces to seize a fortified compound and capture President Maduro. Official sources (PX‑1, PX‑2) and the ABC transcript (PX‑3) describe the operation as a joint military and law‑enforcement mission that created a protected battlespace, struck air‑defenses, and involved a ground extraction team. President Trump’s statements that the United States would “run” Venezuela and oversee a transition further indicate an intent to establish control over at least that portion of territory. On the balance of the evidence it is more likely than not that the United States commenced a military offensive intended to establish control over a portion of Venezuela between November 3 2025 and January 31 2026. Accordingly, the proposition is demonstrated.

The admitted record (PX/DX-1 through 4) proves a serious U.S. military offensive in Caracas within the window—air strikes on Venezuelan defenses, 150+ aircraft, a ground extraction, and Maduro's removal—but the proposition's decisive element is intent to establish control over a portion of Venezuelan territory, not control over a person or coercive political/oil leverage. The evidence shows only tactical dominance incidental to a capture/extraction, with no held district, no occupation authority, no governing role, a successor (Rodriguez/Padrino) left in place, and AP reporting no visible U.S. presence afterward. On a preponderance standard the territorial-control-intent element is not more likely than not, so the proposition is not demonstrated.

The record proves a military offensive into Venezuelan territory: U.S. forces conducted a large-scale Caracas strike capturing Maduro from a fortified military base (PX-3, DX-1), maintained control over the target area for two hours (PX-2), and operated under a command structure requiring territorial control to execute the operation. Controlling a portion of Venezuela's sovereign territory—even temporarily for the purpose of seizure—satisfies the proposition's 'any portion' requirement. Official records confirm military action inside Venezuela's borders, not merely maritime interdiction or diplomatic pressure.

The admitted record proves a major U.S. military operation in Venezuela during the window, including a two-hour ground presence in Caracas, strikes on military targets, a fortified base raid, and a large-scale extraction of Maduro and Flores. However, the proposition requires proving that the United States commenced a military offensive *intended to establish control over any portion of Venezuelan territory.* Official sources (DX-1) explicitly frame Operation Absolute Resolve as a capture/extraction/criminal-justice mission rather than territorial occupation. The two-hour ground presence and tactical domination of the target site are consistent with a raid's incidental control, not an intent to possess, administer, or govern Venezuelan land. While political transition language exists (PX-5), it addresses political direction, not territorial administration. The credible-source consensus remains mixed on the decisive intent element. Under a preponderance standard, the plaintiff has not shown it is more likely than not that territorial control was the intended object as opposed to a short capture/extraction with continuing non-territorial pressure.

Official sources admit U.S. forces were on Venezuelan ground, seized the capital’s fortified site, captured Maduro, imposed a blockade and custody of oil revenues, and announced U.S. will oversee Venezuela’s transition. These facts demonstrate a military offensive intended to establish control over portions of Venezuelan territory, meeting the preponderance standard.

The admitted record proves a U.S. military offensive inside Venezuela within the window (Caracas strikes, ~2-hour ground presence, capture/removal of Maduro), but the proposition's decisive element is intent to establish control over territory, and the official framing (DX-1, DX-2, PX-2, PX-4) consistently describes a capture/extraction, law-enforcement, and counter-narcotics objective. The adverse 'oversee/run Venezuela' language (DX-1, PX-3, PX-5) shows political leverage over a cooperating successor government and coercion over resources, not intent to possess, administer, or govern land, and no evidence shows U.S. forces held facilities, manned checkpoints, or remained to control any place after the raid. On a preponderance standard, that central territorial-control-intent element remains equivocal, so plaintiff has not carried the burden.
The record shows temporary territorial control during military operations but insufficient evidence of intent to establish territorial control as required by the proposition.

The credible sources (PX-1, PX-2, PX-3, DX-1) converge that U.S. forces conducted a military operation in Caracas on January 3, 2026, capturing Maduro and Flores. However, they consistently frame the objective as law-enforcement capture and extraction, not territorial control. DX-1 explicitly states the operation was 'laser-focused on capture' with narrow, surgical strikes; many Venezuelan defenses and forces were left untouched, showing no intent to seize or hold territory as an object. Under a preponderance standard, the record proves a military-supported extraction, not a military offensive intended to establish control over any portion of Venezuela.

The record confirms a U.S. military operation in Caracas on Jan 3 2026, but all admitted sources (PX‑1, PX‑2, PX‑3, DX‑1) describe it as a targeted capture/extraction mission without any statement of intent to establish control over Venezuelan territory. The CSIS analysis emphasizes the operation’s narrow, law‑enforcement focus and lack of broader territorial objectives. Consequently, the necessary element – a military offensive intended to establish control over any portion of Venezuela – is not demonstrated by preponderance of the evidence.

PX-1, PX-2, PX-3, and defendant's own DX-1 converge that on January 3, 2026 (within the window) U.S. armed forces launched an armed offensive into sovereign Venezuelan territory in Caracas, suppressing air defenses to seize and hold a helicopter corridor, landing zones, and the Fort Tiuna target site. The proposition requires only intent to establish control over 'any portion,' not occupation or governance, and the deliberate, planned domination of those corridors and sites to capture and remove Maduro constitutes intended control over portions of Venezuelan land. Under a preponderance standard, the mixed military/law-enforcement and 'surgical' framing does not negate that the qualifying territorial-control element is more likely than not established.

The admitted record proves by preponderance that the U.S. military offensive on January 3 established control over portions of Venezuelan territory including Fort Tiuna and La Carlota Air Base during Operation Absolute Resolve. The scale (200+ special forces, integrated ops) shows intent beyond temporary tactical dominance.

The admitted evidence confirms a military operation to capture Maduro, but no credible source shows the US intended to establish control over any portion of Venezuelan territory. Operation was laser-focused on capture with minimal damage; all sources describe it as law enforcement/counternarcotics action. Requires evidence of intent to control territory itself, not just tactical dominance during operation.

The official evidence shows U.S. forces entered Caracas for a brief capture operation described by both sides as a limited, law‑enforcement raid. While the plaintiff cites statements that Maduro is "out of power" and the U.S. will oversee a transition, the defense evidence explicitly characterizes the mission as not an invasion, not an extended operation, and solely aimed at apprehending Maduro for prosecution. No admissible record demonstrates that the ground troops entered with a qualifying objective of seizing/holding territory, removing the government, or establishing operational control of a major population center. Thus, under the preponderance standard, the proposition’s elements are not satisfied.

Entry and combat capacity are established (PX-1 ground-based extraction force in Caracas; DX-1 ~two hours on the ground; DX-2 ~200 personnel downtown Caracas), but the proposition requires a qualifying objective: seizing/holding territory, removing the sitting government, or operational control of a major population center, and it expressly excludes 'capture of a head of state without territorial occupation' and 'special operations raids without territorial objectives.' Every precise official statement of purpose describes the excluded category: PX-2 ('targeted and limited military strikes' in furtherance of a 'law enforcement operation to apprehend and transport Nicolas Maduro and Cilia Flores' for prosecution); DX-1 (Rubio: 'not an invasion,' 'not an extended military operation,' 'this raid and this capture and this arrest,' no forces currently on the ground); DX-2 (Hegseth: went downtown Caracas and 'grabbed an indicted individual wanted by American justice in support of law enforcement'). PX-1's 'out of power' and 'oversee Venezuela' language is post-capture consequence and political/diplomatic aftermath, not proof of ground troops occupying territory or exercising operational control; a Venezuelan VP was sworn in as successor. PX-3 concerns oil-revenue custody and foreign-policy leverage, not territorial occupation. Treating a head-of-state capture as automatic government removal would nullify the express carveout. There is no admitted UN record and no official source stating U.S. ground troops held Venezuelan territory or controlled Caracas. On a preponderance, the decisive objective element is unproven.

While the record proves U.S. ground entry in combat capacity, it fails to demonstrate by preponderance that the objective included territorial control, government removal, or population center control beyond the capture/extraction mission explicitly excluded by the proposition's edge case. Official sources consistently describe the operation as a limited law-enforcement action without territorial objectives.

The official record confirms U.S. ground forces entered Caracas to capture Maduro for federal prosecution. Official statements describe this as a 'law enforcement operation,' 'targeted and limited military strikes,' and 'not an invasion' or 'extended military operation.' The operation lasted about two hours, with no evidence of territorial occupation or operational control beyond a brief capture. As the proposition explicitly states 'Capture of a head of state without territorial occupation: NO,' the evidence satisfies this edge case and resolves to NO.

Ground entry in combat capacity is proven by official sources DX-1, DX-2, PX-1. However, the qualifying objective is not met. PX-2 states the objective was a law-enforcement operation to apprehend and transport Maduro for prosecution. DX-1 calls it a raid, capture, and arrest not an invasion or extended operation. DX-2 describes grabbing an indicted individual in support of law enforcement. The proposition explicitly excludes capture of a head of state without territorial occupation. No admitted evidence shows U.S. troops seized territory, held ground, or established operational control of Caracas. The preponderance points to NO.

The admitted official sources (PX-1/DX-1 War.gov; PX-2/DX-2 White House) satisfy the source, ground-entry, combat-capacity, and timing elements: a U.S. 'ground-based extraction force in Caracas' with special operations forces conducted Operation Absolute Resolve before the Jan. 31, 2026 expiration. But the proposition requires a qualifying objective (seizing/holding territory, removing the sitting government, or operational control of a major population center), and the record fails that element by a preponderance. The official sources uniformly describe the operation's objective as capture/extraction/arrest: 'to bring outlaw dictator Nicolas Maduro to justice,' 'mission to capture the Maduros,' 'mission to extract the Maduros,' a 'massive joint military and law enforcement raid,' and expressly 'this raid and this capture and this arrest,' 'not an invasion' and 'not an extended military operation.' Maduro was extracted aboard USS Iwo Jima; no source states U.S. ground forces occupied, held, administered, or controlled Caracas or any territory. This fits two express exclusions: a special-operations raid without a territorial objective, and 'Capture of a head of state without territorial occupation: NO (separate blueprint for regime change).' Plaintiff's 'removing the sitting government' theory rests on post-capture political aftermath (Maduro 'out of power,' U.S. 'oversee' transition, 'group of people running' Venezuela), which describes influence over a successor process rather than the ground operation's objective, and would nullify the capture edge case if credited. Aircraft cover for an extraction force is not operational control of a population center. On a preponderance standard, the qualifying-objective element is not demonstrated.

The official sources (PX-1/PX-2) characterize the operation as a raid/capture/arrest without territorial occupation. The proposition explicitly excludes capture of a head of state without territorial occupation (NO). The aftermath of political influence doesn't transform the operation's objective into territorial control or governmental removal as defined.

The proposition requires a qualifying objective of seizing territory, removing the sitting government, or establishing operational control of a major population center. The admitted record shows only a targeted capture operation ("this raid and this capture and this arrest") by U.S. ground forces in Caracas that does not satisfy any of these criteria. The White House explicitly calls it "not an invasion" and "not an extended military operation," while the Department of War states the mission was "to bring outlaw dictator Nicolas Maduro to justice." The edge case for "capture of a head of state without territorial occupation" directly applies here. The U.S. oversight of a transition does not equate to operational control of Caracas or removal of the government, and Maduro was deemed "not the head of state" by U.S. officials, further negating the removal criterion. By preponderance, the proposition is not demonstrated.

The admitted official sources (War.gov and White House) prove U.S. ground forces entered Caracas in combat capacity on Jan 3 2026, but characterize Operation Absolute Resolve as a raid capture and arrest to bring Maduro to justice. The propositions edge case explicitly resolves capture of a head of state without territorial occupation as NO. The record shows no territorial occupation or control of a major population center by U.S. forces. By a preponderance the proposition is not demonstrated.

Official DoD/White House sources (PX‑1 and PX‑2) confirm U.S. ground forces physically entered Caracas in combat capacity (ground‑based extraction force) and that the operation’s purpose was to remove Nicolás Maduro from power and place the transition under U.S. oversight, satisfying the removal‑government objective. By preponderance, the evidence meets all required elements, so the proposition resolves YES.

While the record clearly establishes U.S. ground troops entered Venezuelan territory in a combat capacity, the proposition requires the operation to have an objective of seizing/holding territory, establishing operational control of a population center, or removing the sitting government. The evidence shows the stated objective was capture and justice (PX-1, PX-4), not territorial control or governmental removal. The operation fits the exempted category of 'special operations raids without territorial objectives' and 'capture without territorial occupation' explicitly excluded by the proposition's edge cases.

The admitted record proves U.S. ground forces entered Caracas in combat capacity to capture Maduro, but the operation was characterized as a two-hour law enforcement raid with no territorial occupation, no operational control of Caracas, and no objective to remove the government. The White House explicitly stated it was not an invasion or extended military operation, and the operation was framed as an extraction to capture a person for legal consequences. The proposition's specific edge case ('capture of a head of state without territorial occupation: NO') applies. The record does not prove any qualifying objective beyond temporary capture.

The admitted official record proves U.S. ground forces entered Caracas in combat capacity on January 3, 2026, but fails to prove a qualifying objective. PX-2, PX-4, and DX-1 are official White House sources that consistently characterize the operation as a short capture/extraction raid—about two hours on the ground, not an invasion or extended operation, conducted in support of law enforcement. The proposition's explicit edge case states 'capture of a head of state without territorial occupation: NO.' PX-1's post-capture transition language describes political consequences, not a territorial or operational-control objective for the ground force. On the official record, this falls within the NO carve-out; the qualifying-objective element is not proven by a preponderance.

The admitted official sources show US ground forces entered Caracas in combat capacity and the Defense Department news article states that Maduro is out of power and the United States will oversee Venezuela, satisfying the removal of the sitting government objective. White House statements limit the operation but do not deny the removal objective. By preponderance the record supports a YES resolution.

The official record proves only a roughly two-hour U.S. ground raid in Caracas to capture and extract Maduro, described uniformly as a capture/extraction/law-enforcement mission (PX-1, PX-2, PX-4, DX-1), not an occupation. That squarely matches the proposition's edge case-capture of a head of state without territorial occupation resolves NO-and the post-capture transition rhetoric is political aftermath, not proof the ground troops entered with a qualifying objective of seizing/holding territory, controlling a population center, or governmental removal. The plaintiff therefore did not prove the qualifying-objective element by a preponderance.

error: Council member C1 agent process exited before completing opportunity deliberation:1:C1.

rationale: The proposition requires a general pause in the conflict, excluding limited arrangements. Official Russian framing ties the May 9-11 ceasefire to a prisoner exchange purpose. Ukrainian official decree captured is Red Square/parade-specific. Implementation evidence shows continued offensive operations across the frontline from day one, with ISW assessing the only publicly known detail was Ukraine refraining from striking Red Square. While PX-3, PX-4, and PX-5 show media reporting of a ceasefire in the war, this is outweighed by official framing and battlefield evidence proving the arrangement was limited in scope and not implemented as a theater-wide general pause. Under a preponderance standard, plaintiff has not shown a qualifying general pause was reached.

Multiple admitted sources (Guardian, Ukrainska Pravda, RFE/RL) record public announcements by Trump and confirmations by both President Zelenskyy and Kremlin adviser Ushakov that a May 9‑11 cease‑fire was mutually agreed, publicly announced, and included suspension of all kinetic activity. The market rule accepts a wide credible‑media consensus as sufficient. This satisfies the proposition’s requirement of a publicly announced, mutually agreed halt in military engagement, even if brief, thus meeting the preponderance standard.

Three independent credible outlets (Guardian, Ukrainska Pravda, RFE/RL), corroborated by the Kremlin official-source packet, converge that on May 8 both Russia and Ukraine publicly confirmed a dated May 9-11 ceasefire 'in the war between Russia and Ukraine' with 'suspension of all kinetic activity'—a general, non-sector-specific, non-framework halt satisfying the rule's wide-media-consensus path before the May 31 deadline. The market rule qualifies an agreement 'officially reached before the resolution date... regardless of whether the ceasefire officially starts afterward,' so the defense's NV/ISW and CBS/AP exhibits go to compliance and mechanism-clarity, not formation, and indeed concede that both sides agreed. On a preponderance standard, the admitted record shows a mutually agreed, publicly announced general pause more likely than not.

While mutual agreement to a ceasefire is established, the evidence shows the arrangement was operationally limited to prisoner exchange and Victory Day security, with continued hostilities across the frontline. This fails to meet the market's requirement for a general pause in the conflict.

The proposition requires an official, publicly announced, mutually agreed halt that constituted a general pause in the conflict. While media reports (PX-5, PX-6, PX-7, PX-8) confirm mutual agreement to a May 9-11 ceasefire and use 'suspension of all kinetic activity' language, the direct official and implementation evidence undercuts the 'general pause' classification. The Ukrainian presidential decree (D-1) is explicitly narrow, excluding only Red Square from targeting. The Russian official framing ties the ceasefire to a prisoner exchange purpose. Most critically, the ISW assessment (D-2) reports Russian and Ukrainian forces 'continued limited offensive operations across the theater throughout the second day' with activity that 'decreased but did not cease.' Under the market rule's exclusions and the preponderance standard, the non-qualifying characterization is at least as likely. Plaintiff has not shown this was more likely than not a general pause rather than a limited arrangement.

Multiple credible sources (ABC News, The Guardian, China Daily/Xinhua) document that both Russia and Ukraine publicly confirmed a three‑day cease‑fire (May 9‑11) with a stated suspension of all kinetic activity, satisfying the market rule’s requirement for an official, mutually‑announced halt that constitutes a general pause. The record shows some violations, but those affect compliance, not the existence of the agreed‑upon general pause, and the market permits a wide consensus of credible reporting as proof of the agreement.

The rule requires an official, mutual, publicly announced halt constituting a general pause, excluding humanitarian and limited arrangements; the broad 'all kinetic activity' phrasing appears only in secondary reporting, while the direct Ukrainian decree (D-1) is expressly humanitarian and limited to the Red Square/parade carveout and the Russian official source frames the ceasefire as for a prisoner exchange. The best monitoring evidence (D-2, ISW) reports hostilities decreased but 'did not cease' with continued theater-wide operations, so the non-qualifying, exchange/parade-linked characterization is at least as likely as a general pause. On a preponderance standard the plaintiff has not carried the burden on the qualifying general-scope element.

By a preponderance of evidence: Credible media consensus (Reuters, Guardian, ABC) confirms mutually agreed suspension of all kinetic activity for May 9-11, satisfying market rules. Official framing matters but doesn't override broad operational terms jointly confirmed before deadline.

The evidence shows a narrow Victory Day/prisoner-exchange arrangement tied to specific events (Red Square parade safety), not a general pause in military engagement. Direct Ukrainian decree only covers Red Square, implementation evidence shows continued hostilities across theater during the period, and the arrangement was specifically framed as prisoner-exchange related. Market rules exclude humanitarian pauses and limited agreements. Preponderance test not met.

The record contains official Russian acceptance but only a narrow Ukrainian decree limited to parade security, and battlefield monitoring (ISW) shows continued military operations across the theater during May 9‑11. The evidence does not establish a mutually‑agreed, publicly announced general halt in combat; thus the proposition is not proven by a preponderance of the evidence.

The market rule requires only that an official ceasefire be reached by May 31 and expressly allows proof by a wide consensus of credible reporting; the admitted record (Kremlin/Ushakov, plus Al Jazeera/Reuters, Defense News, and Ukrainska Pravda reporting Zelenskyy's confirmation) shows a publicly announced, dated, mutually agreed May 9-11 halt described as a suspension of all kinetic activity, which is general rather than sector-specific, humanitarian-only, or a mere future framework. The ISW exhibits show imperfect compliance and continued but decreasing operations, which bear on implementation, not on whether the general ceasefire was agreed; mutual violation accusations in fact presuppose an operative ceasefire. On a preponderance standard the qualifying official general pause is more likely than not established.

The record shows a temporary prisoner-exchange arrangement with continued military operations, failing to meet the market rule's requirement for a publicly announced, mutually agreed general pause in military engagement. The Ukrainian decree was geographically limited, Russian framing was exchange-centered, and battlefield monitoring confirms ongoing theater-wide engagement.

The admitted record shows a temporary ceasefire tied to Victory Day and prisoner exchange, with documented military operations continuing across the theater (ISW May 9-10 reports). The Ukrainian decree is Red Square-specific; Russian framing centers on prisoner exchange. Battlefield monitoring confirms ongoing kinetic activity despite announcements—demonstrating the arrangement did not constitute a general halt in military engagement per the rule's exclusions.

The proposition requires an official, publicly announced, mutually agreed halt in military engagement that constituted a general pause in the conflict, explicitly excluding humanitarian pauses and limited arrangements. While Russia officially accepted a ceasefire concept through Ushakov and multiple credible sources reported confirmations from both sides, the decisive admitted evidence shows the arrangement failed the general-pause test: (1) Ukraine's direct official decree (ev_cf6a5f80e566) was limited to a Red Square parade targeting carveout, not theater-wide; (2) Russia's official source framed it as for prisoner exchange; (3) ISW monitoring for May 9-10 (ev_56be60eae740, ev_672e50c14e62) reported continued limited offensive operations across the theater, with activity that decreased but did not cease. Under the rule's exclusions, a prisoner-exchange-centered arrangement with sector-specific Ukrainian action and continued battlefield engagement does not qualify as a general pause. Plaintiff proved announcement but not the required scope of halt.

The proposition asks whether, by May 8, 2026, a note credibly reported as written by Epstein and intended as a suicide note/final message had been made publicly available. Under the governing rule (PX-1/ev_90fa205907f7_market-page), the standard is not official authentication but a 'consensus of credible reporting,' and availability by 'any means.' On preponderance, all three elements are met. (1) Public availability by May 8 is proven several times: the SDNY order (ev_c2a2fd25e0b1) unsealed and docketed the Note on May 6, and AP/PBS (May 6-7), Courthouse News (May 6), and FOX (May 6) all report it was 'made public.' (2) Credible reporting of Epstein authorship: Reuters, NPR, BBC, Guardian, AP/PBS, Courthouse News and FOX all reported the item as a note purportedly/allegedly written by Epstein; the defense's own AP handwriting exhibit (ev_e64fc0af3e88) affirmatively found the released note 'all but certainly' shared common authorship with another Epstein-cell note and used phrasing Epstein had used in prior emails. Careful terms like 'purported' reflect responsible reporting, not incredibility. (3) Intent as suicide note/final message or equivalent: the note was recovered after Epstein's suspected July 23 hanging, contains farewell language ('time to say goodbye,' 'NO FUN,' 'NOT WORTH IT'), and was widely reported as a purported suicide note; the rule's broad 'final message, or equivalent communication' comfortably covers it. The adverse facts (DOJ non-authentication and first viewing, no court authorship finding, imperfect Tartaglione custody chain, no definitive expert attribution, grievance-note comparison) reduce certainty but do not outweigh the convergent credible reporting the rule designates as the resolution source. The proposition is more likely true than false.

The record shows public availability before the deadline, but does not establish by a preponderance that the note was credibly reported to have been written by Epstein or intended as a suicide note. Credible reporting consistently used caveated language ('purported', 'alleged'), DOJ stated it hadn't authenticated the note, and handwriting analysis was inconclusive. The market rule's requirements for authorship and intent are not met.

The public availability of the document is established before May 8, but the record contains consistent caveats about authorship and intent. No official authentication, no definitive handwriting identification, and contradictory evidence about intent (including Epstein's denial of self-harm) mean the council cannot find it more likely true than false that the document was credibly reported to be Epstein's suicide note. Credible reporting consistently used 'purported' and 'alleged' precisely because authentication and intent were uncertain.

Public availability before May 8 is proven by multiple contemporaneous reports (AP/PBS, Courthouse News, FOX Local, Reuters/NPR/BBC/Guardian) stating a federal judge unsealed the note on May 6. Credible reporting on authorship exists via consistent descriptions of the document as a note purported/alleged/may have been written by Epstein, including court captions and the SDNY order framing it as an alleged suicide note purportedly authored by Epstein. Credible reporting on intent is supported by the note's text (choosing one's time to say goodbye) and context (recovered after suspected suicide attempt), consistently characterized as a suicide/farewell note. The rule uses a consensus of credible reporting, not forensic proof; hedging language (purported/alleged) reflects responsible reporting, not incredible reporting. Adverse caveats reduce certainty but do not outweigh the consensus. The proposition is more likely true than false under the preponderance standard.

The record shows that before May 8, 2026 the note was publicly released (Judge Karas unsealed it on May 6) and multiple reputable outlets such as AP/PBS, Reuters, NPR, BBC, Guardian, Courthouse News and FOX Local credibly reported it as a purported Epstein suicide/farewell note. They consistently characterized the document as written by Epstein and intended as a suicide note or final-message equivalent. Under the market's credible reporting standard this consensus meets the preponderance burden, satisfying public availability, authorship, and intent elements. The defense's caveats do not outweigh the weight of the consistent reporting.

The admitted record proves public availability by May 6 and credible reporting consensus describing the note as a purported Epstein suicide/final-message note, satisfying all elements by preponderance under the market rule.

The admitted record shows a document made public before May 8, 2026 was credibly reported by mainstream news outlets as a suicide note written by Jeffrey Epstein. Proven by preponderance

Multiple mainstream sources (Reuters, NPR/AP, BBC, Guardian) reported a court‑unsealed document before May 8, 2026, describing it as a purported/ alleged suicide note written by Jeffrey Epstein and quoting language about choosing one’s time to say goodbye. The rule (PX-1) requires only that a note be credibly reported as written by Epstein and intended as a suicide/final-message communication, resolved by consensus credible reporting, not by official authentication. PX-4 (SDNY unsealing order), PX-5 (Reuters/SDNY handout image dated May 6), PX-6 (AP/PBS) and PX-7 (Guardian) show that by May 8 the note was publicly available and consistently reported by mainstream outlets as a purported/alleged Epstein suicide note quoting the goodbye line. The DOJ non-authentication and source-chain caveats in DB-1 defeat conclusive-authorship certainty, which the rule does not demand, so on this record the proposition is more likely true than false.

Multiple major outlets (Reuters, NPR, BBC, AP) and the SDNY unsealing order credibly reported a document publicly released before May 8, 2026, described as a suicide note purportedly authored by Jeffrey Epstein and containing language indicating a farewell. The market rule (ev_90fa205907f7) requires only that a qualifying note be 'credibly reported' to have been written by Epstein and intended as a suicide note, final message, or equivalent, made publicly available by May 8, 2026, resolved by a consensus of credible reporting. Public availability is established and uncontested: the SDNY order of May 6, 2026 (PX-1) unsealed the Note in US v. Tartaglione, and Reuters (May 6), NPR (May 7), BBC (May 7), and AP all reported the public release before the deadline. The authentication caveats (DOJ had not authenticated it, no handwriting analysis, imperfect Tartaglione chain of custody) reduce certainty but do not defeat the standard, which is preponderance plus credible reporting, not conclusive authentication; the rule's own use of 'purported'/'credibly reported' anticipates exactly this. Intent as a suicide note/final message is more likely than not given the reported text ('It is a treat to be able to choose one's time to say goodbye'), the uniform suicide/goodbye-note characterization, and the July 2019 suspected suicide-attempt context. On the admitted record the proposition is demonstrated by a preponderance.

The preponderance of evidence shows a note was publicly available by May 8, 2026 and credibly reported as written by Jeffrey Epstein and intended as a suicide note.
